- Activity Monitor (ACTMON)
## 9.6 Activity Monitor (ACTMON)
### 9.6.1 Overview
This chapter describes the Activity Monitor (ACTMON) block in the NVIDIA® Orin™ series System- on-Chip (SoC). Activity monitoring is a means to dynamically measure the utilization of various functional units in the system, to help drive software power management policies. For example,
- Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling may be used as a power management mechanism in some
applications. Software uses utilization information from various units to select an efficient voltage and frequency at which the unit should operate, while providing the required performance. Voltage change decisions are handled by this central software agent, whereas the unit frequency can be modulated in one of the following ways:
- No Activity Monitoring
The unit controls its own frequency.
- The device driver controls the unit frequency based on the calculated workload or hints
from a user-level driver.
- Activity Monitoring
- The central Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling (DVFS) agent controls the unit
frequency based on the utilization information from a Central ACTMON. In addition to tracking utilization, activity monitoring also provides hardware mechanism to report monitored activities through interrupts as opposed to polling. The hardware mechanisms include averaging, watermark detection, and histogram hardware, thus reducing software overhead in procuring and tracking the utilization data. The DVFS agent can directly access and use the ACTMONs to compute the target frequency (and consequently, voltage) of each unit for the next sampling interval. The ACTMON acts as a repository for activity indicators from various units, performs averaging of past samples, and indicates any abnormality in activity levels by sending an Interrupt to system software. The monitored units provide their idle/busy signals to the ACTMON block. In the discussion of ACTMON, the following terms are defined:
- Central ACTMON
A module on the AXI2APB bridge within the BPMP cluster. The Central ACTMON contains several ACTMON Instances for monitoring various system resources.
- Unit ACTMON
- The ACTMON inside Cortex-R5 clusters and Host Controller that counts the 1-bit activity
signal generated within the unit it resides in. The Unit ACTMON is responsible for watermark detection and Interrupts generation.

- Activity Monitor (ACTMON)
(Refer to the Cortex-R5 cluster and Host Controller chapters for details of how the 1-bit activity signal is generated.)
- ACTMON Instance (Plug-in)
An instance of either Central ACTMON or Unit ACTMON. Two types of activities are monitored.
- Central Activities
- Memory activities
- Unit Activities
- Unit engine activities and memory bottlenecks
- - - - - -
- AON Cortex-R5
- BPMP Cortex-R5
- SCE Cortex-R5
- RCE Cortex-R5
- DCE Cortex-R5
- APE Cortex-A9
- Unit active signals
- - - - - -
- NVJPG, NVJPG1
- NVENC
- NVDEC
- VIC
- OFA
- PVA
- ACTMON counters are employed to capture activities, from which watermark Interrupts are
generated as result of sudden activity changes for appropriate system responses.
#### 9.6.1.1 Glossary
Note that different names may end up in the same acronym when the entire Orin TRM is put in perspective. The acronyms listed here are within the context of the ACTMON chapter.
- Term
- Definition
- ACTMON
- Activity Monitor
- AFBIF
- AXI-FBIF (AXI to Frame Buffer Interface)
- EWMA
- Exponentially Weighted Moving Average
- FBIF
- Frame Buffer Interface
- PM
- Performance Monitor (inside Cortex-R5 cluster and responsible for the Unit ACTMONs)

- Activity Monitor (ACTMON)
- Term
- Definition
- Sampling Interval
A software-programmable time interval during which ACTMON hardware samples the selected activity indicator once to accumulate in its activity counter
#### 9.6.1.2 Relevant Chapters
- Address Map
- Always-On (AON) Cluster and SPE
- Audio Processing Engine (APE)
- Boot and Power Management Processor (BPMP)
- Clock Controller and Reset (CAR)
- Control BackBone (CBB)
- CPU Complex (CCPLEX)
- Interrupt Controllers
- Programmable Video Accelerator (PVA)
- Real-time Camera Engine (RCE)
- Safety Cluster Engine (SCE)
- Timers
- Video and Image Compositor (VIC)
#### 9.6.1.3 Features
Following is highlight of the ACTMON features. One 32-bit counter for each signaling device to accumulate activity counts. Running average computation for each counter over its past N values (N = 128). Watermark breach detection logic in each counter to detect any abrupt change in utilization. Activity report through Interrupts.
- Exponentially Weighted Moving Average (EWMA) for each central ACTMON counter to
provide the running average of its past K (programmable) values. DBB-related ACTMON, Central ACTMON outside of MSS, monitoring only DBB signals. PVA ACTMON, Unit ACTMON inside PVA module.
### 9.6.2 Functional Description
- The ACTMON hardware accumulates selected activity indicators over a programmable period
known as the sample period. The ACTMON uses the accumulated activity value in two ways:

- Activity Monitor (ACTMON)
- First, it compares the accumulated activity count with two software-programmable
thresholds (one high and one low). If the monitored activity falls outside the thresholds, ACTMON hardware generates an Interrupt to send to the ACTMON driver.
- Secondly, ACTMON feeds the accumulated activity count to an IIR filter to compute an
- Exponentially Weighted Moving Average (EWMA) of the activity count to effectively measure
the "average load" of the monitored unit.
- The ACTMON hardware maintains a second pair of programmable thresholds to use as the upper
and lower boundary of the EWMA. If this moving average changes by the software-programmed amount (threshold), ACTMON hardware generates an Interrupt to send to the ACTMON driver.
**Figure 9.65 ACTMON Hardware Diagram**
- The diagram above illustrates the Orin ACTMON hardware. The ACTMON Wrapper is a software-
defined encompassment of multiple ACTMON modules/Instances (up to 8). Each ACTMON module in an ACTMON Wrapper with dedicated hardware and registers is assigned to monitor just one Activity indicator. The Global Control Registers for the sample period and Interrupt enable/status are common for all ACTMON Modules in an ACTMON Wrapper.
- Referring to the ACTMON Block Diagram below, the activity input is counted by the Activity
counter, ACTMON_XXX Monitor Counter register, that is reset at the end of each sample period. Software configuration allows Interrupt generation for the condition of counter overflow or at the end of each sample period. This cumulative counter, however, keeps running, even after a sample period ends, until it rolls over. Software has the option to generate an Interrupt when it rolls over for the first time (CARRY) and the second time (OVERFLOW).

- Activity Monitor (ACTMON)
At the end of a sample period, the sample count value is sent to a first order IIR filter to provide an Exponentially Weighted Moving Average statistics. Software can also be configured to generate an Interrupt whenever a set of programmable thresholds are breached. All the Interrupts are finally routed to one Interrupt line. Software reads the Interrupt status registers to know the cause of Interrupt. In addition, software has the option to mask (enable/ disable) Interrupts.
**Figure 9.66 ACTMON Block Diagram**
The figure below shows the high-level connectivity interface for the Central ACTMON unit in the
- BPMP cluster. (Refer to the BPMP chapter of this TRM for more details.)
- NV_ACTMON_MC is the ACTMON Wrapper encompasses seven ACTMON Instances. This along with
other glue logic like APB Slave and Reset Synchronizer constitutes the Central ACTMON. The Activity signals from Memory Controllers (MC) are all 16 bits wide (1 bit from each of the 16 MC channels) but each ACTMON Instance can only take a “1” bit activity signal as input. Thus, the NV_ACTMON_MC Wrapper needs to take care of activity aggregation (not shown in the diagram below). See Activity Aggregation below.

- Activity Monitor (ACTMON)
**Figure 9.67 Central ACTMON Connection**
The table below summarizes the activities monitored in Orin.
- Table 9.61 Activity Monitors (ACTMONs)
- Module
- Central ACTMON Counter Description (Counter Width = 32 Bits)
- MC-ALL ACTMON
- Number of Memory Controller (MC) clock cycles when any (including CCPLEX) memory access
activity is detected. The MC toggles a signal to ACTMON every 'W' cycles.
- MC-CPU ACTMON
Number of MC clock cycles when any memory access activity from CCPLEX is detected. MC toggles a signal to ACTMON every 'W' cycles.
- TSA_A ACTMON
Number of MC clock cycles when any memory access activity from TSA_A is detected. MC toggles a signal to the ACTMON every 'W' cycles.
- TSA_B ACTMON
Number of MC clock cycles when any memory access activity from TSA_B is detected. MC toggles a signal to the ACTMON every 'W' cycles.

- Activity Monitor (ACTMON)
- Module
- Central ACTMON Counter Description (Counter Width = 32 Bits)
- TSA_C ACTMON
Number of MC clock cycles when any memory access activity from TSA_C is detected. MC toggles a signal to the ACTMON every 'W' cycles.
- DUAL-MCCIF-TSA
- ACTMON
- Number of MC clock cycles when any memory access activity from DUAL-MCCIF-TSA clients is
detected. MC toggles a signal to ACTMON every ‘W’ cycles.
- TSA_RD ACTMON
Number of MC clock cycles when any memory access activity from TSA_RD clients is detected. MC toggles a signal to ACTMON every ‘W’ cycles. The table above assumes that the following units either run at a fixed frequency or have their own frequency management based on a fixed workload, so they do not need ACTMON support.
- VI/CSI (fixed)
- Display (based on workload; no monitors needed)
- Host (fixed)
- ISP (based on workload)
- NVENC (based on input buffer)
- SEC (fixed frequency)
- USB3 – Falcon (based on workload)

Unit-ACTMON registers are in the parent device’s address space. Hence, they should be accessed through the parent device’s MMIO path.

##### 9.6.2.1.1 Interrupts
- Table 9.63 Interrupt Interface
- Port Name
I/O
- Width
- Description
actmon_intr_set
- Out
- This signal tells the parent that ACTMON Interrupt need
to be set. It keeps asserted until software clears the status in ACTMON. (Parent refers to the unit inside which the ACTMON is instantiated.) Each Unit ACTMON has a single Interrupt status bit in the parent module which is set by this signal and cleared by software.
##### 9.6.2.1.2 Interrupt Handling
The Interrupts raised by all ACTMON Instances within the Wrapper are aggregated and sent to the parent device with one Interrupt status bit for the Wrapper. The parent device then sends the aggregated Interrupt to the software.
- Step 1: Host Controller Interrupt triggered
Step 2: Software reads Host Controller Interrupt status register and decides which activity monitor triggers Interrupt.
- Step 3: Software reads each ACTMON Interrupt status to decide which source triggered the
Interrupt.
- Step 4: Software clears the corresponding ACTMON and Host Controller Interrupt status bit
and takes necessary actions.
#### 9.6.2.2 Central ACTMON Module
The central ACTMON module comprises several central ACTMON Instances.
- Table 9.64 Clock Source for Activity Monitors
- ACTMON
- Refclk Source
- Frequency
- Central ACTMON
- Oscillator
## 19.2 MHz
- NV_timertick - The central ACTMON module uses the mS and µs ticks from NV_timers block
to keep track of sample periods.
##### 9.6.2.2.1 Accounting for Variable ACTMON and Device Clocks
The main purpose of Central Activity Monitors (Central ACTMONs) is to compute the utilization per unit time. This requires a constant time base that can only be provided by a constant reference clock (refClk).

- Activity Monitor (ACTMON)
Variation between refClk and device clock could lead to errors in counting. The following describes the issues relating to this clock variation and how they are solved: Accounting for different frequencies:
- Since the signaling devices may be operating at different frequencies, the ACTMON
counts need to be scaled appropriately to compute true utilization. For this, each counter is associated with a COUNT_WEIGHT value (denoted by 'W') which is the ratio of the device clock and the constant ACTMON reference clock (refClk). At the end of each sample period, the counter value is scaled by COUNT_WEIGHT to account for the frequency difference. 'W' is statically selected for each client such that it maps the client frequency range to a "reasonable" monitor output change. Accounting for pulse loss from high-frequency devices:
- Devices, such as MC, NVDEC, NVENC, and NVJPG, operate at much higher frequencies
than the ACTMON clock. Since ACTMON functions based on the idle/active pulses from these devices, a number of pulses are dropped as result of the slower ACTMON clock. To solve this problem, the device, instead of sending active/idle for each clock, toggles a signal for every 'N' active pulses of the device, where 'N' is the ratio of the device frequency to ACTMON frequency and needs to be programmed by software into the
- ACTMON and corresponding devices. Effectively, the idle/active pulses of these high-
frequency devices are reduced by a factor of 'N.' Using the 'N'-factor-reduced idle/active pulses, which is guaranteed to be slower than the ACTMON clock, no pulses are missed.
**Figure 9.68 Unit_ACTMON Handling High-Frequency Activity Signals**

- Activity Monitor (ACTMON)
#### 9.6.2.3 Handling Overflow
Saturation of the counter leads to counter overflow and results in an Interrupt to the system software. The software is expected to clear the counter upon completion of servicing the Interrupt for continual use.
#### 9.6.2.4 Features to Aid DVFS
The Central ACTMON module provides the following hardware support for DVFS:
- Watermark Generation
- Each counter in the ACTMON unit is associated with an upper and lower watermark
register to track any sudden abnormalities in utilization, e.g., rapidly varying workloads. A programmable number of watermark breaches is used to trigger the generation of an Interrupt to system software.
- Exponentially Weighted Moving Average (EWMA)
- An EWMA is maintained for each Central ACTMON counter to keep a running average of
the past 'K' (programmable) counter values. It is implemented as a 1st-order IIR filter using the following formula:
- At = K * Ct + (1 - K) * At-1, where
- At is the running average,
K = 2-( K_VAL+1) (default = 2-7), Ct is the current count value. Upper and lower watermarks are also provided for this EWMA.
- Cumulative Counter
- Each Central ACTMON has a cumulative counter register to keep track of the activity
across sample periods. This counter register is updated continuously, not cleared/reset at sample period boundaries. The count in this cumulative counter register can be polled by the software (mostly BPMP firmware) at frequent intervals (< 10 mS granularity) to understand the activity trends.
- Each ACTMON Instance also has a cumulative counter register to store the cumulative
activity count value. This counter register is incremented at each active cycle of the activity signal to accumulate the activity continuously through sample periods, not cleared/reset at sample boundaries. This counter register is NOT compared against any thresholds to generate any Interrupts.

- Activity Monitor (ACTMON)
- Each ACTMON has a counter that accumulates the activity signal over a programmable
sample period. At the end of the sample period, the counter value is latched into a register.
- At any instant, software reads this register to receive the activity count for the most
recently completed sample period.
#### 9.6.2.5 Unit ACTMONs for Multimedia Engines (NVJPG, NVENC, NVENC1,
- NVDEC, NVDEC1, and VIC)
In Orin, two Unit ACTMONs, one for Engine Activity and the other for Memory Bottlenecks, are assigned for each multimedia engine (NVJPG, NVENC, NVDEC, VIC) to allow system software to concurrently study engine load and memory bottlenecks. Orin system software has the ability to aggregate signals inside these multimedia modules before exporting to Unit ACTMON.
**Figure 9.69 Multimedia Engine ACTMON**
The four signals on the right side of the diagram above are described below: Engine_Active goes HIGH for the ACTIVE cycle, i.e., when any of the subunits of the engine is active.

- Activity Monitor (ACTMON)
- Stalled goes HIGH when the engine is unable to issue a request to memory, i.e., a STALLED
cycle.
- Delay goes HIGH when the engine is waiting for a read-response or write-acknowledge from
memory. Starved goes HIGH when engine’s internal input memory buffers are empty. The default configuration can be:
- Engine Active = (Engine_active & !Starved)
- Memory Bottleneck = (Engine_active & Starved)
- At Power On Reset (POR)
1.
- The unit exports the two signals: Engine Active and Memory Bottleneck. This requires that
the AFBIF infrastructure to support the Boolean logic aggregation then export the two signals to the Host Controller, where the two ACTMON Instances are located.
- VIC provides the Starved signal. NVJPG, NVENC, and NVDEC units do not generate the
Starved signal. 2.
#### 9.6.2.6 Unit ACTMON for BPMP/SCE/RCE/AON Cortex-R5 Processor
One Unit ACTMON is instantiated for each of the Cortex-R5 processors in BPMP, SCE, RCE, and AON cluster. These Unit ACTMONs are intended to aid software with load-based frequency scaling of the processor clock. It can also count selected signals from the Cortex-R5 Event Bus to identify Memory-Mapped Read and Write (MMIO) latency limits. To enable common design and verification, these Unit ACTMONs are instantiated as part of the common Performance Monitor (PM) module inside the individual Cortex-R5 cluster. The active signal for these Unit ACTMONs can be sourced from the following:
- WFEPIPESTOPPED
- WFIPIPESTOPPED
- CLKSTOPPED
- Selected signals from the Cortex-R5’s Event Bus
For details of these signals and how they can be combined to generate the Activity signal for the Unit ACTMON, see the BPMP chapter of this TRM. The Activity signal is accumulated using a down-counter and when it reaches zero, the signal to the Unit ACTMON is toggled. The Unit ACTMON runs on the refClk just like the Central ACTMONs and we expect it to get reset when the PM module (within which it sits) gets reset.

- Activity Monitor (ACTMON)
The Unit ACTMON's Interrupt is sent to PM module which adds an additional Interrupt line for the ACTMON.
#### 9.6.2.7 Activity Aggregation
The memory controller has 16 N-Bit signals to ACTMON (where N = 16 = number of channels) and the ACTMON logic combines them to update the counter as follows: For each of the MC_CPU, MC_ALL, DBB NoC related ACTMON Instances @ refClk: if (neither signal toggles)
- No change
else if (any 1 signal toggles)
- Increment by W
else if (any 2 signals toggle)
- Increment by 2*W
else if (any 3 signals toggle)
- Increment by 3*W
else if (any 4 signals toggle)
- Increment by 4*W
else if (any 5 signals toggle)
- Increment by 5*W
else if (any 6 signals toggle)
- Increment by 6*W
else if (any 7 signals toggle)
- Increment by 7*W
else if (any 8 signals toggle)
- Increment by 8*W
else if (any 9 signals toggle)
- Increment by 9*W
else if (any 10 signals toggle)
- Increment by 10*W
else if (any 11 signals toggle)
- Increment by 11*W
else if (any 12 signals toggle)
- Increment by 12*W
else if (any 13 signals toggle)
- Increment by 13*W
else if (any 14 signals toggle)
- Increment by 14*W
else if (any 15 signals toggle)
- Increment by 15*W
else if (any 16 signals toggle)
- Increment by 16*W
Where W is the weight (VAL bits) programmed in the COUNT_WEIGHT register.

- Activity Monitor (ACTMON)
### 9.6.3 Programming Guidelines
This section provides an overview of the ACTMON register fields.
- Central ACTMON module contains a set of global registers which are common to all central
ACTMON Instances and some registers which are replicated for each ACTMON. The following are global registers, common to all central activity monitors.
- ACTMON Global Control
- ACTMON Global Interrupt Enable
- ACTMON Global Interrupt Status
The registers marked by XXX are replicated for each central ACTMON.
#### 9.6.3.1 Global Registers
ACTMONs only support 32-bit accesses. Byte accesses are not supported. Refer to ACTMON Registers for detailed descriptions of the global registers.
- ACTMON Global Control Register (ACTMON_MCGLB_CTRL_0)
- If the programmed value of SAMPLE_PERIOD is N,
the sample period duration would be (N+1) units (in mS/sample_tick or µs/sample_tick). This is the sample period for all ACTMON Instances within the central ACTMON module.
- If the SOURCE is selected as TICK, i.e., ACTMON reference clock,
sample period = (N+1) * (256 or 65356 depending on SAMPLE_TICK field) * unit, where unit
- ACTMON reference clock period.
- ACTMON Global Interrupt Enable Register (ACTMON_MC_GLB_INT_EN_0)
- For each Interrupt Enable bit: 1 for each ACTMON Instance (Instance 0 - 7) in the
ACTMON Wrapper.
- ACTMON Global Interrupt Status Register (ACTMON_MC_GLB_INT_STATUS_0)
- For each Interrupt Status bit: 1 for each ACTMON Instance (Instance 0 - 7) in the
ACTMON Wrapper.
#### 9.6.3.2 Central ACTMON Instance Registers
- The XXX in the ACTMON Instance register names is any of the ACTMON module names, i.e.,
MC_CPU, MC_ALL, TSA_A, TSA_B, TSA_C, DUAL_MCCIF_TSA, and TSA_RD_AVG_COUNT.

- Activity Monitor (ACTMON)
- The upper and lower watermarks of instantaneous and average count can be enabled
simultaneously by software.
- ACTMON_XXX Control Register (ACTMON_XXX_CTRL_0)
- ACTMON_XXX Interrupt Enable Register (ACTMON_XXX_INTR_ENABLE_0)
- Software needs to ensure that whenever the ACTMON is in the disabled mode, all the
corresponding Interrupt enables are set to disabled mode as well. In addition, software must ensure that the Interrupt status register is cleared before the ACTMON is re-enabled from a disabled state.
- ACTMON_XXX Interrupt Status Register (ACTMON_XXX_INTR_STATUS_0)
- ACTMON_XXX Upper Watermark Register (ACTMON_XXX_UPPER_WMARK_0)
This software-programmed register is the upper watermark for the ACTMON count value.
- ACTMON_XXX Lower Watermark Register (ACTMON_XXX_LOWER_WMARK_0)
This software-programmed register is the lower watermark for the ACTMON count value.
- ACTMON_XXX Average Upper Watermark Register (ACTMON_XXX_AVG_UPPER_WMARK_0)
This software-programmed register is the upper watermark for the ACTMON EWMA value.
- ACTMON_XXX Average Lower Watermark Register (ACTMON_XXX_AVG_LOWER_WMARK_0)
This software-programmed register is the lower watermark for the ACTMON EWMA value.
- ACTMON_XXX Init Average Value Register (ACTMON_XXX_INIT_AVG_0)
- This software-programmed register is the initial value of EWMA, corresponding to A0 in
the IIR filter formula.
- ACTMON_XXX Moving Average Register (ACTMON_XXX_AVG_COUNT_0)
- This is the EWMA count value. This is updated at end of sample period based on IIR filter
formula.
- ACTMON_XXX Monitor Counter (ACTMON_XXX_COUNT_0)
This is the ACTMON counter value. Note that this register is not updated at each clock. There is a separate counter which increments for each cycle the activity signal is ACTIVE.
- At end of a sample period, the value of this counter is scaled by the COUNT_WEIGHT and
then updated into this register. If read in middle of a sample window, the readback value is the old sample’s monitor value.
- ACTMON_XXX Count Weight Register (ACTMON_XXX_COUNT_WEIGHT_0)
- This register holds the COUNT_WEIGHT. The activity accumulated over the past sample
period is scaled by this weight and then updated into the Monitor Counter register.
- ACTMON_XXX Cumulative Count Register (ACTMON_XXX_CUMULATIVE_COUNT_0)

- ACTMON Registers
This register contains the cumulative counter value.

## 9.7 Hardware Synchronization Primitives (HSP)
### 9.7.1 Overview
This chapter describes various modules used to allow multiple processors to share resources and communicate together. These synchronization elements are intended for software level interprocessor synchronization and messaging, especially between different Arm processors. This chapter does not discuss the synchronization elements used between a processor and a peripheral, or the mechanisms used between software threads in the same processor cluster.
#### 9.7.1.1 Rationale
The Arm® instructions supporting exclusive access (LDREX/STREX, SWAP, etc.) can only be used inside a coherency domain and so cannot be used between different processor clusters in the SoC. This hardware therefore provides a set of hardware synchronization primitives for interprocessor synchronization. There are also times when the cores inside the CCPLEX clusters do not operate in an SMP fashion. This is required particularly during some power management sequences with caches and/or MMU disabled. Hardware synchronization may again be required during these periods if exclusive access to a shared resource is required.
#### 9.7.1.2 Use Cases
- Interprocessor Communication (IPC) protocols use hardware synchronization mechanisms,
especially the doorbells, when operating between two processors not in an SMP relationship.

- Hardware Synchronization Primitives (HSP)
#### 9.7.1.3 Shared Mailboxes
The shared mailboxes support a single-writer and single-reader model.
#### 9.7.1.4 Shared Semaphores
The shared semaphores are formed by three registers, each 32-bits wide: A register showing the current value of the semaphore
- Two write-only registers to set and clear individual semaphore bits
#### 9.7.1.5 Arbitrated Semaphores
The arbitrated semaphores are true mutex binary semaphores that also include state information to avoid software use of a busy spin loop if so desired.
#### 9.7.1.6 Doorbells
The doorbells implement a simple mechanism to support IPC protocols. Each doorbell is one-to-one associated with a given destination processor, and the assertion of the doorbell signal indicates to the destination processor that at least one other processor requires attention, typically because the source processor posted a message in memory.
- The doorbell mechanism uses a source-tracking approach to aggregate multiple requests for
attention into a single register, simplifying the software communication model: the target processor identifies the set of pending requests through a single register read.
### 9.7.2 Functional Description
- The SoC implements an IP block that encapsulates the multiple hardware synchronization
primitives, and is accessed via an APB bus.

- Hardware Synchronization Primitives (HSP)
**Figure 9.70 Decomposition of the Hardware Synchronization Primitive**
The HSP module is instantiated multiple times in Orin, together with the number of instances of the different primitives inside each instance. In the following table: nSM is the number of Shared Mailboxes nAS is the number of Arbitrated Semaphores nSS is the number of Shared Semaphores nDB is the number of Doorbells nSI is the number of Shared Interrupts (see below)
- Red shows which HSPs are redundant
- Type shows the two possible HSP configurations

- Hardware Synchronization Primitives (HSP)
- Table 9.65 HSP Module Instantiation
- Instance
nSM nAS nSS nDB nSI
- Red
- Type
- Description
- TOP0
- Yes
- TOP0 instance additionally has
- Arbitrated Semaphores and
- Doorbells with each doorbell having
an identified destination.
- TOP1
- Yes
- Consists a total of eight
bidirectional mailboxes at TOP, same as R5 HSP to limit verification effort.
- TOP2
- Yes
- Same as TOP1 Flavor and is mainly
used for FSI<->CCPLEX communication.
- BPMP
- Yes
- All Cortex-R5 clusters, the A9
cluster, FSI Cluster, TOP1, and TOP2 instances use a similarly configured HSP.
- AON

The Shared Mailboxes, Arbitrated Semaphores, and Shared Semaphores are always replicated per security level, and each HSP supports two different security levels (TrustZone® or non-TrustZone), so that nSM, nAS, and nSS are always a multiple of two to allow differentiation between two security levels. The doorbell logic is only duplicated for the CPU where one doorbell can be used to ping the TrustZone secure software without interference from TrustZone non-secure software or exposing any information to the TrustZone non-secure software.
- Note: RCE_HSP1, RCE_HSP2, DCE_HSP1, and DCE_HSP2 instances exist to maintain common
design for SCE, RCE and DCE. Doorbells are system resources, instantiated once into a top level HSP. Doorbell interrupts are sent to specific targets:

- Hardware Synchronization Primitives (HSP)
- Two doorbells connected to the LIC, and from there to the CCPLEX GIC, are normally mapped
to TrustZone secure and non-secure. (The CCPLEX CPU doorbell signals could be made directly visible to the CCPMU, this is not done in Orin.)
- One doorbell directly connected to the CCPMU inside CCPLEX (the MCE used to
communicate with BPMP). Five doorbells for AON, BPMP, SCE, DCE, and RCE R5 clusters. Each doorbell connected to VIC inside a Cortex-R5 cluster. One doorbell connected to the PSC interrupt controller.
#### 9.7.2.1 Sub-Unit Descriptions
The HSP module has these main blocks:
- APB termination
- Common registers and interrupt crossbar
- Shared mailboxes
- Shared semaphores
- Arbitrated semaphores
- Doorbells
##### 9.7.2.1.1 APB Termination
The APB termination logic is a slave APB that transforms incoming APB transactions into local register read and write operations.
##### 9.7.2.1.2 Common Registers and Interrupt Crossbar
The common registers and interrupt crossbar block contains the interrupt routing logic that takes the set of local interrupts and generates a set of shared interrupts, connected to an interrupt controller associated with the instance: LIC for TOP0 and TOP1 HSP. LIC, FSI GIC, FSI AVIC, and FSI HSM for TOP2_HSP. One shared interrupt to the local AVIC and four shared interrupts to LIC for AON, SCE, RCE, DCE, and PVA HSP instances. Local AVIC, FSI GIC, FSI HSM for SCE_HSP1 and SCE_HSP2 instances. Local AVIC for RCE_HSP1, RCE_HSP2, DCE_HSP1, and DCE_HSP2 instances. Local GIC and AVIC for FSI HSP instance. The LIC and local VIC for HSP instantiated in BPMP. The A9 GIC for HSP instantiated inside the APE.

- Hardware Synchronization Primitives (HSP)
Note: Number of shared interrupts (nSI) is eight for all HSP instances but only five of them are used in AON_HSP, BPMP_HSP, SCE_HSP, RCE_HSP, DCE_HSP, PVA_HSP, and APE_HSP instances.
##### 9.7.2.1.3 Shared Mailboxes
- HSP supports two types of shared mailboxes, 32-bit and 128-bit. Each of the eight shared
mailboxes in HSP can be used as either 32-bit shared mailbox or 128-bit mailbox. Usage of 32-bit Mailbox and 128-bit Mailbox is mutually exclusive as they share same Full and Empty interrupts. 32-bit Shared Mailbox The 32-bit shared mailboxes are word width registers with a very simple interrupt mechanism controlled by the MSB. Their simplest operating model is single writer/single reader. Each 32-bit shared mailbox has the following resources: A 32-bit register. Full and empty interrupts controlled by the MSB value of the register. The MSB is interpreted as a Valid bit, so the full interrupt is the value of the MSB and the empty interrupt is the inverted value of the MSB. Enable bits for full and empty interrupts. 128-bit Shared Mailbox To add support for Mailbox data width>31-bit, 128-bit Mailbox support is added in Orin:
- Current Software is not impacted and can choose between 32-bit mailbox vs 128-bit mailbox
as per the use-cases.
- Five new registers are added for 128-bit mailbox support: one register for TAG and four
registers of each 32-bit for mailbox data. Mailbox full interrupt is asserted when the TAG field of the TAG register is one and mailbox empty bit is asserted when the TAG is set to 0 in TAG register. Mailbox data can be written into the four new mailbox data registers as per the required data size.
- The number of shared mailboxes is extended to support more than one pair of communicating
processors and virtualization requirements. There are 24 unidirectional mailboxes (eight for TOP0, eight for TOP1, eight for TOP2) at TOP_HSP and eight unidirectional mailboxes local to each Cortex-R5, FSI, and A9 cluster: APE, BPMP, AON, SCE, RCE, DCE, PVA0, and FSI. Additionally, SCE cluster has SCE_HSP1 and SCE_HSP2 instances each with eight unidirectional mailboxes, RCE cluster has RCE_HSP1 and RCE_HSP2 instances each with eight unidirectional mailboxes and DCE cluster has DCE_HSP1 and DCE_HSP2 instances each with eight unidirectional mailboxes. The expected usage model of shared mailboxes is:
- Dedicated to a specific consumer, as it is generally easier to share the input port

- Hardware Synchronization Primitives (HSP)
- Dedicated to a bidirectional IPC method, in which case shared mailboxes are allocated as a
pair, one per direction
- Preferably, the IPC is between one producer and one consumer to avoid resource conflicts
This is not always possible in multithreaded or multiprocessor use cases, in which either: A single threaded software agent is used as a resource manager per direction, arbitrating, and dispatching message. This assumes that all software producers are able to synchronize using software mechanisms, i.e., not shared mailboxes, and the same for all software consumers. This is a viable model for the BPMP ↔ CCPLEX IPC.
- The input and output semaphores are used for resource sharing
No easy way to operate a mailbox with agents at different levels of security on the same port.
- If the IPC requires a mix of security levels and QoS per level, the IPC must use as many
mailboxes as required to achieve the required QoS.
- Targeting (very) short messages, not bulk transfer. The assumption is that the message can
identify a descriptor for a larger structure when needed, possibly via an address offset against some known base or an index in a list or whatever is convenient for software. Shared mailboxes are not optimized for bandwidth.
##### 9.7.2.1.4 Shared Semaphores
- The shared semaphores are registers with associated SET/CLR addresses to allow easy
manipulation of individual bits inside them, i.e., without the need for a RMW operation. There is no hardware arbitration, so software must statically allocate the ownership of individual semaphores for correct operation. The shared semaphores are formed by three registers, each 32-bits wide:
- Read-write register showing the current value of the semaphore
- Two write-only registers to set and clear individual semaphore bits
- These semaphores can be used for synchronizing processors acting in a producer/consumer
relationship, i.e., a pair of them can be used to implement either a two- or four-way handshake. Correct operation of the shared semaphores require to statically allocate an owner for each bit, because it is impossible to implement an atomic test and set (or similar operation) given the register interface. It also means that calling them "semaphores" is a bit misleading; they are atomic set and atomic clear registers.
##### 9.7.2.1.5 Arbitrated Semaphores
The arbitrated semaphores are true binary semaphores that also include state information to avoid a busy spin loop if so wanted. The arbitrated semaphores support two sides, denoted 0 and 1, and arbitrates between requests coming from each side. In essence, the arbitrated semaphores are built in the following way, all registers being 32-bits wide:

- Hardware Synchronization Primitives (HSP)
A set of read only flags representing ownership (SMP_GNT_ST), showing ownership of the semaphore, one register per side (0 and 1). A set of write only trigger request flags (SMP_GET), a processor can only acquire a resource (represented by a bit in the status word) through setting a corresponding request flag. The request is blocked until the resource is not owned by a different processor. Currently, request flags are only cleared by acquiring the resource. Again, there is one register per side (0 and 1). A set of clear flags (SMP_PUT), a processor relinquishes ownership by writing a one bit corresponding to the shared resource (read-write). A set of request status flags (SMP_REQ_ST) showing the current pending requests (read only) One companion set of registers is defined, with one register per side (0 and 1). Enable register (INT_EN), enabling the generation of an interrupt if the corresponding status bit toggles. Storing the request information instead of using write triggers allows the following implementation to avoid the typical busy spin lock loop and the corresponding waste of bandwidth and power.
- EnableOwnershipInterrupt (i);
- RequestResource (i);
- WaitForInterrupt();
##### 9.7.2.1.6 Doorbell Logic
A doorbell allows a set of source agents in the chip to request the attention of a specified target agent. The doorbell logic presents two logically separate interfaces: 1. The trigger interface can be used by any master in the system; the doorbell logic uses source ID tracking to recognize the exact source. The control interface is normally configured for use by the target agent only. 2. Note that the doorbell SLICE1 registers support masters with masterId>15. The trigger interface also distinguishes between TrustZone secure and not secure security levels. The doorbell includes one bit per possible source, per supported security level. Writing to the trigger interface sets a corresponding bit as shown in the pseudo code below (for one doorbell):
- SourceID = f(pUSER) // extract source ID for APB USER field
- If SourceID <= 15
- If pns == TZ_SECURE: Slice0[Map_0][SourceID] = 1b
- If pns == TZ_NOT_SECURE: Slice0[Map_1][SourceID] = 1b
- Else if ((SourceId >=16) && (SourceId < 32))
- If pns == TZ_SECURE: Slice1[Map_0][SourceID] = 1b
- If pns == TZ_NOT_SECURE: Slice1[Map_1][SourceID] = 1b

- Hardware Synchronization Primitives (HSP)
The control logic uses security aware registers to control whose agent has access to it, with the following set of registers:
- Standard security configuration register to specify the exact security model
- Bitmap indicating which sources can raise the doorbell signal and at which security level
- Status/control register indicating the set of sources with pending flags (clear on write)
- Status/control register indicating the raw bitmap (clear on write)
The control logic then generates one doorbell signal per doorbell. The doorbell signals are:
- Exposed as a port with a direct connection to the target processor cluster interrupt
controller
- VIC inside a Cortex-R5 cluster
- GIC inside the APE cluster
LIC for CCPLEX, and from there directly to the GIC, as the GIC is essentially transparent for the CCPLEX path
- CCPLEX power management
- CCPMU register for the CCPMU doorbell
Exposed to the interrupt crossbar logic in the HSP, so that it can be mapped as a HSP shared interrupt, allowing the doorbell to be routed to a different target. The operation of one doorbell is illustrated in the figure below:
**Figure 9.71 Doorbell Logic**

- Hardware Synchronization Primitives (HSP)
Doorbells are identified by numbers, with the following mapping between a doorbell number and its intended destination.
- Table 9.66 Mapping between Doorbell Number and Usage
- Doorbell
- Target
- CCPMU
- CCPLEX TrustZone not secure
- CCPLEX TrustZone secure
- BPMP
- AON
- SCE
- APE
- RCE
- DCE
- PSC
#### 9.7.2.2 Multiple Processors Access to Shared Resources
The SoC does not support full coherency mechanisms, so an IPC protocol must tackle ordering and visibility issues. The protocol uses a doorbell interrupt to get attention and a standard structure of circular buffers plus associated read/write pointers in shared memory (internal or external). With this model, the expected software sequence looks like the following:
##### 9.7.2.2.1 Source Processor
1. 2. 3. 4. 5. 6. 7.
- Writes a set of messages with known format in shared memory circular buffer structure
- If the shared memory is cacheable, flushes its cache to make the messages globally visible
- Issues a DMB to ensure that pointer and message are ordered
- Writes the value of the write pointer at known location in shared memory
- If the shared memory is cacheable, flushes its cache
- Issues a DMB to ensure ordering between pointer and interrupt
- Writes to a doorbell register in MMIO space to trigger the interrupt to the destination
##### 9.7.2.2.2 Destination Processor
1. 2.
- Receives doorbell interrupt and enters Interrupt Service Routine
Reads the associated doorbell status register to identify the set of source processors with pending messages

- Hardware Synchronization Primitives (HSP)
3.
- Cache invalidates the address used by the write pointer for the source processor circular
buffer
- Issues a DMB
- Read the write pointer
Cache invalidates the addresses used by the set of messages identified by the write pointer
- Issues a DMB
- Reads the set of messages
4. 5. 6. 7. 8.
#### 9.7.2.3 Using Doorbells for Handshake During Boot Sequence
During the boot sequence, the doorbells are used to synchronize the boot sequence across multiple processors. Each doorbell allows for a full handshake between two processors, denoted as B (normally BPMP) and D (normally CCPLEX power management). The handshake makes sure that D executes a specific task A at a time controlled by B and works like this. B performs all tasks that must be done before task A can be done. When everything is ready, B signals to D that it can proceed by ringing D doorbell (i.e., writing to the doorbell trigger register). B can continue working on other tasks not dependent on task A completion. When it reaches a point that requires task A to be complete, B check the doorbell pending status. If the doorbell is still asserted, B waits for completion by polling on the doorbell status. D performs all tasks that must be done before task A can be done. When everything is ready D checks if its doorbell is asserted. If not, it polls on its doorbell until assertion. D performs task A, then signals completion to B by clearing the doorbell (i.e., by writing to the doorbell pending status). Clearing the doorbell is acting as an acknowledge by D.
- When B sees the doorbell pending status deasserted, it knows that task A is complete and
can now execute tasks dependent on task A completion.
#### 9.7.2.4 Shared Interrupts Configuration
Each shared interrupt is the aggregate of the selected bits; in other words, each shared interrupt is driven by the same structure of AND followed by OR used in LIC. The figure below shows the logic for the Top0 HSP instance.

- Hardware Synchronization Primitives (HSP)
**Figure 9.72 Shared Interrupt Logic**
Assume that software wants to map the shared interrupts as follows:
- Shared0 is a (aggregated) mailbox empty, so IE0 = 0xFF
- Shared1 is a (aggregated) mailbox full, so IE1 = 0xFF << 8
- Shared2 is Arbitrated Semaphore 0, and Arbitrated Semaphore 1 is not used, so IE2 = 0x1 <<
#### 9.7.2.5 Programming Model
The programming model is register based.
### 9.7.3 Programming Guidelines
#### 9.7.3.1 Shared Mailboxes
A shared mailbox serves a unidirectional communication between a producer and a consumer. The two masters agree upon a mailbox to use, and which master is the producer and which master is the consumer. In the following sequences, it is assumed that shared mailbox {sm} is selected.

- Hardware Synchronization Primitives (HSP)
##### 9.7.3.1.1 Producer Sending a Message
- Make sure the selected shared mailbox is empty by checking the TAG field of register
- HSP_SHRD_MBOX_MBOX_{sm}_SHRD_MBOX_0 or HSP_SHRD_MBOX_MBOX_{sm}
_SHRD_MBOX_TYPE1_TAG_0 register for 128-bit mailbox.
- Write the message in the DATA field of register HSP_SHRD_MBOX_MBOX_{sm}
_SHRD_MBOX_0 or HSP_SHRD_MBOX_MBOX_{sm}_SHRD_MBOX_TYPE1_DATA*_0 registers for 128-bit mailbox. Set the TAG field of the register to 1. 1. 2.
##### 9.7.3.1.2 Consumer Receiving a Message
1.
- Read register HSP_SHRD_MBOX_MBOX_{sm}
_SHRD_MBOX_0 or HSP_SHRD_MBOX_MBOX_{sm}_SHRD_MBOX_TYPE1_TAG_0 register for 128-bit mailbox. If TAG field is 1, then the DATA field carries the message. After consuming the message in the mailbox, set the TAG field of the register to 0. The DATA field is don't care and can be written with any value. 2.
#### 9.7.3.2 Shared Semaphores
A producer/consumer pair uses shared semaphore to synchronize management of a shared resource. Each bit in a shared semaphore register represents a shared semaphore. The producer and consumer select a shared semaphore to use (a particular shared semaphore register and a particular bit) and agree on their roles. In the following sequences, we assume bit {b} of shared semaphore register {ss} is selected.
##### 9.7.3.2.1 Producer
1.
- Make sure the shared semaphore is empty by checking that bit {b} in HSP_SHRD_SEM_{ss}
_SHRD_SMP_STA_0 is 0. The Producer writes to the shared resource. Indicate the data in the shared resource is ready for consumption by writing 1 to bit {b} in HSP_SHRD_SEM_{ss}_SHRD_SMP_STA_SET_0. 2. 3.
##### 9.7.3.2.2 Consumer
1.
- The shared resource is ready for consumption when reading HSP_SHRD_SEM_{ss}
_SHRD_SMP_STA_0 return value 1 in bit {b}. The Consumer consumes the data in the shared resource.
- Indicate that the shared resource is available for new data by writing 1 to bit {b} in
HSP_SHRD_SEM_{ss}_SHRD_SMP_STA_CLR_0. 2. 3.

- Hardware Synchronization Primitives (HSP)
#### 9.7.3.3 Arbitrated Semaphores
Each arbitrated semaphore is accessed through two register sets, indexed by 0 and 1; one set for each master sharing the arbitrated semaphore. Each bit in the arbitrated semaphore register corresponds to an arbitrated semaphore. The two masters agree on an arbitrated semaphore to use (a particular arbitrated semaphore register and a particular bit), and agree on which master takes register set 0 and which master takes register set 1. In the following sequences, we assume bit {b} of the arbitrated semaphore register {as} is selected.
##### 9.7.3.3.1 Requesting Semaphore with Interrupt
- Enable the ownership interrupt by setting the corresponding bit in HSP_ARB_SEM{0,1}_{as}
_INT_EN_0. Set the corresponding bit in HSP_ARB_SEM{0,1}_{as}_SMP_GET_0 to 1. Wait for the interrupt. When that happens, the semaphore is acquired. 1. 2. 3.
##### 9.7.3.3.2 Requesting Semaphore with Busy Spin Lock
Set bit {b} in HSP_ARB_SEM{0,1}..{as}_SMP_GET_0 to 1.
- Poll register HSP_ARB_SEM{0,1}..{as}_SMP_GNT_ST_0. When the bit {b} is 1, the semaphore
is acquired. 1. 2.
##### 9.7.3.3.3 Relinquishing Semaphore
Set bit {b} in HSP_ARB_SEM{0,1}_{as}_SMP_PUT_0 to 1. 1.
#### 9.7.3.4 Doorbells
A triggering master can ask for attention from the receiving master by ringing the doorbell of the receiving master. In the following sequences, we assume {db} is the doorbell index of the receiving master. There is a dedicated doorbell for each master in TOP0_HSP.
##### 9.7.3.4.1 Ringing a Doorbell
Check that the receiving master receives the doorbell by ensuring that the bit corresponds to the triggering master and its security level in HSP_DBELL_<db>_ENABLE_0 for masters with MasterID 0-15 and HSP_DBELL_<db>_ENABLE_SLICE1_0 for masters with MasterID 16-31. Write to HSP_DBELL_{db}_TRIGGER_0. Any value can be written. 1. 2.

- HSP Registers
##### 9.7.3.4.2 Answering a Doorbell
After servicing a doorbell, the receiving master clears the doorbell from a particular master and security level by writing 1 to the corresponding bit in HSP_DBELL_{db}_PENDING_0 registers for masters with MasterId 0-15 and HSP_DBELL_<db>_PENDING_SLICE1_0 for masters with MasterID 16-31.
### 9.7.4 HSP Registers
The set of registers is split across the different modules, each set share a common base. Each module has a number of 64-KiB pages to help in virtualization in Orin and all of the modules expose their registers via an APB interface. Each submodule in HSP has its own set of 64-KiB pages plus an extra 64-KiB page for the common registers. The different windows are placed one after the other in this order:
- One 64-KiB page for the common registers
nSM/2 64-KiB pages for the shared mailboxes, grouped in pairs to support bidirectional communication. The number of mailboxes in a given HSP must be even. nSS 64-KiB pages for the shared semaphores nAS 64-KiB pages for the arbitrated semaphores One 64-KiB page for the doorbells. All these are referred to a base address shown as HSP_{inst}_BASE in this document, where {inst} identifies the specific HSP instance. All transactions sent to a given module must be terminated, if an address is not assigned or if the access violates some access restrictions, an error response is sent and the access does not affect any state outside of the APB interface itself.
#### 9.7.4.1 Common Registers
Common registers are used for two independent reasons:
- For truly common functionality
To increase the functionality while keeping the legacy architecture. This is done for security handling. It is recommended for some modules are treated as one security group, with the security handling performing in a wrapper that instantiates the legacy code. 1. 2. The common registers offset is from HSP_{inst}_BASE. The common registers consist of Security Control Registers (SCRs) and HSP Interrupt registers:
- HSP_SCR_SS_{ss}_REG_0
- HSP_SCR_AS_{as}_REG_0
- HSP_SCR_SM_{sm}_REG_0

- HSP Registers
- HSP_SCR_DBELL_{db}_REG_0
- HSP_INT_SCR_SCR_C0_REG_0
- HSP_INT_IE{i}_0 (register array)
- HSP_INT_IV_0
- HSP_INT_IR_0
#### 9.7.4.2 HSP Interrupt Registers
The mapping of the HSP internal interrupts is defined as follows:
- Bits[00 + [nSM-1:0]] are the empty interrupts of the nSM instantiated shared mailboxes
- Bits[08 + [nSM-1:0]] are the full interrupts of the nSM instantiated shared mailboxes
- Bits[16 + [nDB-1:0]] are the interrupts of the nDB instantiated doorbells
Bits[ 28 + [nAS-1:0]] are the grant interrupts of the nAS instantiated arbitrated semaphores Bits not defined by the mapping above are marked reserved and not physically present. This is true for both IE{i} and IR registers. Note that this mapping means that nSM, nDB cannot exceed eight and nAS cannot exceed four, for a given HSP instance. These registers have the same bit mapping, shown in the following table.
- Table 9.67 Format of the IE{i} and IR Interrupt Registers
- Bit
- Reset
- Description
31:30 0x0 smp1_gnt_enable/valid/asserted 29:28 0x0 smp0_gnt_enable/valid/asserted 25:16 0x0 dbell_enable/valid/asserted 15:8 0x0 mbox_full_enable/valid/asserted 7:0 0x0 mbox_empty_enable/valid/asserted
##### 9.7.4.2.1 HSP_INT{i}_IE_0
This is an array of eight identical register entries. This array controls the routing of interrupt {i}, a bit mask indicating which of the internal interrupts is propagated to external interrupt {i}, {i} in [0:nSI-1].
- ExternalInterrupts[i] = |(IE{i} & InternalInterrupts}
See the Format of the IE{i} and IR Interrupt Registers table for the organization of the register fields.
- Offset: 0x100..0x11f | Read/Write: R/W | SCR Protection: SCR_C0_REG_0 | Reset: 0x00000000

- HSP Registers
##### 9.7.4.2.2 HSP_INT_IV_0
This register contains the currently asserted shared interrupts, ExternalInterrupts. Each bit of IV is mapped to the corresponding shared interrupt.
- Offset: 0x300 | Read/Write: RO | SCR Protection: SCR_C0_REG_0 | Reset: 0x00000000
##### 9.7.4.2.3 HSP_INT_IR_0
This register indicates the currently asserted internal interrupts, InternalInterrupts. See the Format of the IE{i} and IR Interrupt Registers table for the organization of the register fields.
- Offset: 0x304 | Read/Write: RO | SCR Protection: SCR_C0_REG_0 | Reset: 0x000000ff
##### 9.7.4.2.4 HSP_INT_HSP_CLK_OVR_0
Offset: 0x30c | Read/Write: R/W | Reset: 0x00000000 (0bxxxx,xxxx,xxxx,xxxx,xxxx,xxxx,xxxx,xxx0)
- Bit
- Reset
- Description
0x0 hsp_clk_ovr_on
##### 9.7.4.2.5 HSP_INT_DIMENSIONING_0
Offset: 0x380 | Read/Write: RO | Reset: 0x000XXXXX (0bxxxx,xxxx,xxxx,xxxx,xxxx,xxxx,xxxx,xxxx)
- Bit
- Reset
- Description
19:16 X nSI: Number of shared interrupts in this instance 15:12 X nDB: Number of doorbells in this instance 11:8 X nAS: Number of arbitrated semaphores in this instance 7:4 X nSS: Number of shared semaphores in this instance 3:0 X nSM: Number of shared mailboxes in this instance
- Shared Mailbox Registers
Shared mailboxes are placed 32 KiB apart, so that a pair is mapped in a 64 KiB page. The base for a given mailbox is SM{sm}BASE{sm} = HSP{inst}_BASE + 64 KiB + {sm} * 32 KiB.
- Note: Offset mentioned below is from SM{0}_BASE

- HSP Registers
32-bit Shared Mailbox Registers
##### 9.7.4.2.6 HSP_SHRD_MBOX_MBOX_{sm}_SHRD_MBOX_0
Software-controlled mailbox registers. There are eight shared mailbox registers, where {sm} = 0 through 7. Offset: 0x0 + (sm * 0x8000) | Read/Write: R/W | SCR Protection: SM_{sm}_REG_0 | Reset: 0x00000000 (0b0000,0000,0000,0000,0000,0000,0000,0000)
- Bit
- Reset
- Description
- TAG: This bit acts as a Valid bit for the mailbox. The mailbox is deemed full
when TAG = 1b and empty when TAG = 0b, with the status also reflected in corresponding interrupts. The producer writes TAG to 1b to signal the availability of a message; the consumer writes TAG to 0b to signal that the message has been fully handled. 30:0
- DATA: Value exchanged between producer and consumer. No hardware
semantic.
##### 9.7.4.2.7 HSP_SHRD_MBOX_MBOX_{sm}_SHRD_MBOX_FULL_INT_IE_0
Offset: 0x4 + (sm * 0x8000) | Read/Write: R/W | Parity Protection: N | SCR Protection: SM_{sm} _REG_0 | Reset: 0x00000001 (0bxxxx,xxxx,xxxx,xxxx,xxxx,xxxx,xxxx,xxx1)
- Bit
- Reset

0x1
- ENABLE: Mailbox full interrupt enable

- Bit
- Reset

0x1
- ENABLE: Mailbox empty interrupt enable
128-bit Shared Mailbox Registers
##### 9.7.4.2.9 HSP_SHRD_MBOX_MBOX_{sm}_SHRD_MBOX_TYPE1_TAG
Offset: 0x40 + (sm * 0x8000) | Read/Write: R/W | Parity Protection: N | SCR Protection: SM_{sm} _REG_0 | Reset: 0x00000000 (0b0000,0000,0000,0000,0000,0000,0000,0000)

- HSP Registers
- Bits
- Reset

- TAG: The bit acts as a Valid bit for the 128-bit mailbox. The mailbox is deemed
full when TAG = 1b and empty when TAG = 0b, with the status also reflected in corresponding interrupts. The producer writes TAG to 1b to signal the availability of a message, the consumer write TAG to 0b to signal that the message has been fully handled.
##### 9.7.4.2.10 HSP_SHRD_MBOX_MBOX_{sm}_SHRD_MBOX_TYPE1_DATA0
Offset: 0x48 + (sm * 0x8000) | Read/Write: R/W | Parity Protection: N | SCR Protection: SM_{sm} _REG_0 | Reset: 0x00000000 (0b0000,0000,0000,0000,0000,0000,0000,0000)

#### 9.7.4.3 Shared Semaphore Registers
Each shared semaphore instance has an associated page starting at SS{ss}BASE = HSP{inst}_BASE + (1 + nSM/2) * 64 KiB + {ss} * 64 KiB.
- Note: Offset mentioned below is from SS{0}_BASE
##### 9.7.4.3.1 HSP_SHRD_SEM_{ss}_SHRD_SMP_STA_0
There are eight shared semaphore status registers, where {ss} is 0 through 7. Even though we have eight shared semaphore registers, only 0-3 are valid as maximum number of SS is four for Orin. This register gives the current status for the shared semaphore instance. Offset: 0x000 + (ss * 0x10000) | Read/Write: RO | SCR Protection: SS_{ss}_REG_0 | Reset: 0x00000000
- Bit
- Reset
- Description
31:0 SMP: Current value. Each bit is an individual semaphore.
##### 9.7.4.3.2 HSP_SHRD_SEM_{ss}_SHRD_SMP_STA_SET_0
There are eight shared semaphore set registers, where {ss} is 0 through 7. Set the bits in this register to update the current value. Offset: 0x004 + (ss * 0x10000) | Read/Write: WO | SCR Protection: SS_{ss}_REG_0 | Reset: NA
- Bit
- Reset
- Description
31:0
- NA
SMP_SET: Writing bit i to 1b sets semaphore bit i. Undefined for reads.
##### 9.7.4.3.3 HSP_SHRD_SEM_{ss}_SHRD_SMP_STA_CLR_0
There are eight shared semaphore clear registers, where {ss} is 0 through 7. Clear the bits in this register to update the current value. Offset: 0x008 + (ss * 0x10000) | Read/Write: WO | SCR Protection: SS_{ss}_REG_0 | Reset: NA
- Bit
- Reset
- Description
31:0
- NA
SMP_CLR: Writing bit i to 1b clears semaphore bit i. Undefined for reads.

- HSP Registers
#### 9.7.4.4 Arbitrated Semaphore Registers
Arbitrated semaphore instance {as} has a register range starting at AS{as}BASE = HSP{inst}_BASE + (1 + nSM/2 + nSS) * 64 KiB + {as} * 64 KB. Each arbitrated semaphore instance contains two apertures, identified as {a} in {0,1}, corresponding to two different owners. The owner associated with an aperture is defined by software convention. The following table shows the base offset according to arbitrated semaphore instance and aperture.
- Note: Offset mentioned below is from AS{0}_BASE
- Table 9.68 Base Offsets of Arbitrated Semaphore Registers
- Semaphore Instance {as}
- Base Offset
- Aperture 0 {a} = 0
- Aperture 1 {a} = 1
0x00 0x20 0x10000 0x10020 0x20000 0x20020 0x30000 0x30020
##### 9.7.4.4.1 HSP_ARB_SEM{a}_{as}_SMP_GNT_ST_0
This register indicates current ownership; each bit is an individual semaphore. There are four
- Arbitrated Semaphore Grant Status registers, where {as} = semaphore 0 through 3 and {a} =
aperture 0 or 1. Offset: Base | Read/Write: RO | SCR Protection: AS_{as}_REG_0 | Reset: 0x00000000 (0b0000,0000,0000,0000,0000,0000,0000,0000)
- Bit
- Reset
- Description
31:0
- STATUS: Bit i at 1 indicates aperture{a} is the current owner of resource i
##### 9.7.4.4.2 HSP_ARB_SEM{a}_{as}_SMP_GET_0
There are four Arbitrated Semaphore Get registers, where {as} = s emaphore 0 through 3 and {a} = aperture 0 or 1. Even though we have four Arbitrated semaphore registers, only 0-1 are valid as maximum number of AS is two for Orin. Use this register to make a request for ownership.
- Offset: Base + 0x4 | Read/Write: WO | SCR Protection: AS_{as}_REG_0 | Reset: 0x00000000
(0b0000,0000,0000,0000,0000,0000,0000,0000)

- HSP Registers
- Bit
- Res
et
- Description
31:
- NA
GET: Writing bit i to 1b is a request for ownership of resource i from aperture {a}. All pending requests are accumulated in an internal register visible as HSP_ARB_SEM{a}_{as}_REQ_ST_0. Requests cannot be canceled; a request bit gets cleared when ownership is acquired.
##### 9.7.4.4.3 HSP_ARB_SEM{a}_{as}_SMP_PUT_0
There are four Arbitrated Semaphore Put registers, where {as} = semaphore 0 through 3 and {a} = aperture 0 or 1. Use this register to relinquish ownership.
- Offset: Base + 0x8 | Read/Write: WO | SCR Protection: AS_{as}_REG_0 | Reset: 0x00000000
(0b0000,0000,0000,0000,0000,0000,0000,0000)
- Bit
- Reset
- Description
31:0
- NA
PUT: Writing bit i to 1b relinquishes ownership of resource i from aperture {a}.
##### 9.7.4.4.4 HSP_ARB_SEM{a}_{as}_SMP_REQ_ST_0
There are four Arbitrated Semaphore Request registers, where {as} = semaphore 0 through 3 and {a} = aperture 0 or 1. This register indicates if there are any pending requests. Offset: Base + 0xc | Read/Write: RO | SCR Protection: AS_{as}_REG_0 | Reset: 0x00000000 (0b0000,0000,0000,0000,0000,0000,0000,0000)
- Bit
- Reset
- Description
31:0 REQ: Bitmap of pending requests from aperture {a}.
##### 9.7.4.4.5 HSP_ARB_SEM{a}_{as}_SMP_INT_EN_0
There are four Arbitrated Semaphore Interrupt Enable registers, where {as} = semaphore 0 through 3 and {a} = aperture 0 or 1. Offset: Base + 0x10 | Read/Write: R/W | SCR Protection: AS_{as}_REG_0 | Reset: 0x00000000 (0b0000,0000,0000,0000,0000,0000,0000,0000)
- Bit
- Reset
- Description
31:0
- EN: Interrupt enable. The interrupt is active when resource i is granted to
aperture {a} and the corresponding enable bit is 1.
##### 9.7.4.4.6 Doorbell Registers
All doorbell registers are in a single page, doorbell {db} has a register range starting at DB{db}BASE
- HSP{inst}_BASE + (1 + nSM/2 + nSS + nAS) * 64 KiB + {db} * 0x100.

- HSP Registers
In the Enable, Raw, and Pending registers, each field is defined as a bitmap, where each bit is associated with a master ID. Only the bits associated with valid Master ID are guaranteed to be present. Two of these are packed per register to provide fast access; each map is denoted as MAP{sl}. The variable {sl} takes values 0 (TrustZone secure) and 1 (TrustZone non-secure).
**Figure 9.73 Format for Doorbell Registers Using Bitmaps**
- Note: Offset mentioned below is from DB{0}_BASE
##### 9.7.4.4.7 HSP_DBELL_{db}_TRIGGER_0
In this write-only register, set a pending flag based on the source ID and the security level of the transaction. There are 10 doorbell trigger registers, where {db} is a value from 0 to 9.
- Offset: 0x0 + (db * 0x100) | Read/Write: WO | SCR Protection: None | Reset: NA
- Bit
- Reset
- Description
31:0
- NA
- DUMMY: Any write to this register sets bit Raw_{reg_slice}{db}_{sl}[{src}],
where {reg_slice} = 0 for master ID 0-15 and {reg_slice} = 1 for master ID 16-31 {src} = master ID {s1} = Map0, for master ID 0-15, TrustZone secure {s1} = Map1, for master ID 0-15, TrustZone non-secure {s1} = Map2, for master ID 16-31, TrustZone secure {s1} = Map3, for master ID 16-31, TrustZone non-secure

- HSP Registers

This register indicates which pending flags can raise the doorbell signal. There are ten doorbell enable registers, where {db} is a value from 0 to 9. doorbell{db}_{sl} where {reg_slice} = 0 for master ID 0-15 and {reg_slice} = 1 for master ID 16-31.
- This field is valid when {sl} = Map1, indicating the master is
TrustZone non-secure). 15:0
- MAP0: Which bits in Raw_{reg_slice}{db}_{sl} can raise signal
doorbell{db}_{sl} where {reg_slice} = 0 for master ID 0-15 and {reg_slice} = 1 for master ID 16-31.
- This field is valid when {sl} = Map0, indicating the master is
TrustZone secure).

This register indicates which flags have been set as a result of a write to the {db}_TRIGGER register. This register is used for MasterIDs <16(0 to 15).

##### 9.7.4.4.12 HSP_DBELL_{db}_PENDING_0
This register indicates which flags are set and enabled, equivalently the input to the OR reduce function that controls the signal doorbell_{db}. The read value for {i} is not guaranteed after execution of an operation on {i}. This register is used for MasterIDs <16(0 to 15). Offset: 0xc + (db * 0x100) | Read/Write: RWC | SCR Protection: DB_{db}_REG_0 | Reset: 0x00000000 (0b0000,0000,0000,0000,0000,0000,0000,0000)
- Bit
- Rese
t
- Description
31:1 MAP1: Which enabled flags are currently set. Writing 1 to a bit clears the equivalent bit in the HSP_DBELL_{db}_RAW_0 register, and thus implicitly clears the same bit in this register. This field is valid when {sl} = Map1, indicating the master is TrustZone non-secure). 15:0 MAP0: Which enabled flags are currently set. Writing 1 to a bit clears the equivalent bit in the HSP_DBELL_{db}_RAW_0 register, and thus implicitly clears the same bit in this register. This field is valid when {sl} = Map0, indicating the master is TrustZone secure).

This register indicates which flags are set and enabled, equivalently the input to the OR reduce function that controls the signal doorbell_{db}. The read value for {i} is not guaranteed after execution of an operation on {i}. This register is used for 15 < MasterIDs <32(16 to 31).

the equivalent bit in the HSP_DBELL_{db}_RAW_SLICE1_0 register, and thus implicitly clears the same bit in this register. This field is valid when {sl} = Map3, indicating the master is TrustZone non-secure). 15:0 0x0
- MAP0: Which enabled flags are currently set. Writing 1 to a bit clears

#### 9.7.4.5 Invalid Registers
HSP, like other blocks, will normally create an APB slave error when invalid registers are accessed. However, certain addresses do not do this, as follows: Read and write accesses to the following invalid register addresses do not result in APB slave error response. 0x03c5_0018, 0x03c5_001c, 0x03c5_0020, 0x03c5_0024, 0x03c5_0028, 0x03c5_002c, 0x03c7_0058, 0x03c7_005c, 0x03d5_0020, 0x03d5_0024, 0x03d5_0028, 0x03d5_002c, 0x0165_0020, 0x0165_0024, 0x0165_0028, 0x0165_002c Note that ranges are mentioned only for TOP0, TOP1, and TOP2_HSP. Writing and reading to the invalid register addresses above has no side effect on HSP functionality, but failure to report an error could mask programming errors so HSP users should be aware of this.

- Thermal Sensor and Thermal Throttling Controller
## 9.8 Thermal Sensor and Thermal Throttling
- Controller
### 9.8.1 Overview
This section describes the thermal sensor and thermal throttling controller. It provides information on thermal sensing and thermal event detection. It also details the thermal sensor and temperature sensor calibration registers. The functions of the thermal throttling controller (SOC_THERM) include the following:
- Thermal Sensing Logic (SOC_THERM_tsensor): Accesses, captures, and processes data from
thermal sensors which are placed in multiple locations around the SoC.
- Thermal Event Detection (SOC_THERM_thermalctl): Provides multiple software configurable
thermal thresholds per sensor. Thermal threshold crossings can be configured to raise interrupts, alert hardware safety monitor (HSM), or trigger a hardware throttling response.
- Electrical Event Detection (SOC_THERM_edp): Accesses configurable over-current (OC) input
pins via GPIO which can be used to trigger throttle responses. These pins might be wired to signals from the PMIC or battery monitors.
- Throttling Controller (SOC_THERM_throttlectl): For each of the events that can trigger
throttle response, SOC_THERM provides configuration of that throttling response. Light/ medium/heavy throttle depth and target throttling domain (ex., CPU, GPU) are the configuration options.
#### 9.8.1.1 Thermal Sensor Cells (TSENSE_U3_A, TSENSE_BJT_U3_A)
Thermal sensors are used to constantly monitor the temperature on the chip. Sensors are placed across the die to gauge the temperature of the whole chip.

- Thermal Sensor and Thermal Throttling Controller
#### 9.8.1.2 Thermal Event Detection (SOC_THERM_thermctl)
- SOC_THERM_thermctl monitors temperatures coming from the SOC_THERM_tsensor and takes
configurable actions at configurable temperature thresholds. The available actions are:
- Generating an interrupt
- Asserting one or more hardware thermal throttling indicators to SOC_THERM_throttlectl
- Alerting HSM
- Invoking thermtrip to shut down the system
There are essentially four major categories:
- Group-temperature-based thermal actions
1.
- Generic software trip
Heavy hardware throttling, asserted by rising temperature.
- HSM Alerts
2. HSM hot alert, asserted by rising temperature. HSM cold alert, asserted by falling temperature. Tj_max-based shutdown triggered by rising temperature, sent to PMC, HSM, and PSC. Tj_min-based shutdown triggered by falling temperature sent to PMC, HSM, and PSC. 3. 4. Actions are only taken for thresholds which are enabled and have a valid temperature input.
- Group-Temperature-Based – Category 1
The group-temperature-based thermal actions (category 1) are organized as a two-dimensional array – thermal groups (horizontally) and response types (vertically). Each intersection of a group and response type has a monitor. Each monitor includes its own thermal threshold, and all the monitors act independently of each other. The following options are available for each monitor in category 1: EN – should the monitor operate or not? A monitor will operate if this bit is enabled AND the temperature input is valid.
- UP_THRESH – A temperature threshold. For hardware throttling monitors, temperature rising
beyond this threshold causes the monitor to assert its hardware throttling output.
- DN_THRESH – A temperature threshold. For hardware throttling monitors, temperature falling
below this threshold causes the monitor to deassert its hardware throttling output. If the down interrupt is enabled, the down interrupt is asserted.
- INTR_UP – should the monitor raise an interrupt when temperature rises above UP?
- INTR_DN – should the monitor raise an interrupt when temperature falls below DN?
A monitor includes two separate Finite State Machines (FSMs) for generating the up and down interrupts. The state machines implement hysteresis. So, for example, once a down interrupt has been asserted and cleared it won’t be reasserted until after the temperature has risen above UP.

- Thermal Sensor and Thermal Throttling Controller
Similarly, once an up interrupt has been asserted and cleared it won’t be reasserted until after temperature has fallen below DN. Software can force a transition back to the idle by writing to the monitor’s control register.
- Conceptually, a monitor asserts its output indicator based on the state of the FSM which
generates the up interrupt. Consequently, the monitor cannot request cold throttling (i.e., throttle below a particular temperature). If cold throttling is required, it must be implemented in software.
- HSM Alerts – Category 2
The HSM alerts, unlike the group-temperature-based thermal actions, only use Tj_max and Tj_min respectively. They are not replicated per group, since HSM does not need to know which group is getting too hot or too cold.
- Other than not being replicated per group, the HSM hot alert works the same as the ones in
category 1 and use the same kind of FSM. The options available for HSM cold alert monitors are very similar, but the output is asserted on falling temperature instead of rising temperature. The behavior of the UP_THRESH and DN_THRESH changes for the HSM cold alert monitor instances:
- UP_THRESH – A temperature threshold. Temperature rising beyond this threshold causes the
monitor to deassert the HSM cold alert output. If the up interrupt is enabled, the up interrupt is asserted.
- DN_THRESH – A temperature threshold. Temperature falling below this threshold causes the
monitor to assert the HSM cold alert output. If the down interrupt is enabled, the down interrupt is asserted.
- Tj_max / Tj_min Shutdown – Categories 3 and 4
- The thermal response logic for categories 3 and 4, which respectively correspond to high
temperature and low temperature shutdown, is much simpler. Each has an enable toggle and a single temperature value. If the temperature exceeds the overtemperature limit while the trip is enabled and has a valid temperature reading, the chip is shut down. The same goes for the under- temperature limit, except it triggers then the temperature goes below that programmed limit. SOC_THERM implements the thresholding but relies on PMC to implement the shutdown. Thermtrip is the ultimate backstop for excessively high or excessively low temperatures. It should only happen in truly exceptional circumstances (when normal thermal management fails to control temperatures). A thermtrip shutdown is NOT graceful; it may cause storage media corruption and it provides no mechanism for alerting the user prior to the shutdown. The probability of corrupting storage on a thermtrip may be low, but it is non-zero. The only way to mitigate the issue is to avoid a thermtrip shutdown in the first place.
##### 9.8.1.2.1 Interrupt Handling
- The SOC_THERM thermal interrupt is the aggregation of N interrupt event sources. N is the
product of X groups, two levels, and two directions (up and down) plus interrupts for HSM hot alert and HSM cold alert. Event aggregation and software interrupt management is managed via an

- Thermal Sensor and Thermal Throttling Controller
interrupt status register INTR_STATUS and an interrupt enable register pair INTR_EN / INTR_DIS. The status field is set, regardless of the state of the enables, when temperature breaches the corresponding threshold. Software can read to understand which group and which type to service.
#### 9.8.1.3 Electrical Event Detection (SOC_THERM_edp)
- SOC_THERM_edp centralizes a few mechanisms for detecting and responding to externally
signaled electrical events, usually over-current events. SOC_THERM_edp can be configured to raise interrupts when these events occur. It can also assert a throttle indicator to SOC_THERM_throttlectl for each event. The assertion of a throttle indicator may lead to hardware throttling of CPU or GPU clocks.
##### 9.8.1.3.1 External Over-Current Detection
Platform power delivery hardware has knowledge of over-current or voltage-droop events which cannot be easily detected by the SoC. SOC_THERM accepts multiple signals from platform level hardware indicating that an event is occurring. These are known as OC alarms because they often indicate an over-current event. Software can configure the SOC_THERM response to a particular OC alarm being asserted. In particular, software can configure SOC_THERM to initiate throttling in response to an OC alarm assertion. SOC_THERM hardware can detect the signal and throttle much faster than software could detect and respond to a GPIO interrupt. SOC_THERM provides the following set of capabilities for each over-current alarm:
- Sticky and Brief Throttling Modes: Software can configure the OC alarm response to be
either sticky or brief. 1. Sticky Throttle Mode:
- Applicability: This is used for OC events that need a longer-term frequency adjustment
(for e.g., ac-adapter unplug event).
- Behavior: On the assertion of over-current signal, hardware asserts the corresponding
- OC_ALERT signal to engage throttling. Hardware sends an interrupt to software and
remains in the throttled state until software responds.
- Software Setup: The amount of throttling applied on an over-current event is
preprogrammed by software as appropriate for the type of over-current event that are being detected. The throttling point may be fixed or may need to be updated along with standard DFS clock changes.
- Software Response to Over-current Event: Events requiring sticky throttling are typically
those that require a longer-term adjustment to clock frequencies. After an over-current event, software reprograms clocks to appropriate levels, disengages throttling, and re- arms the over-current feature with new settings. Note that re-arming may first depend on detection of an over-current deassertion event (e.g., if over-current signal is really an
- AC-mode signal). These events may also require software to switch to more conservative
DVFS and EDP tables to prevent repeated occurrences of this event. - - - - Brief Throttle Mode:

- Thermal Sensor and Thermal Throttling Controller
-
- Applicability: This is used for transient OC events that need brief frequency adjustments
(e.g., voltage droop detected by a voltage comparator).
- Behavior: On the assertion of over-current signal, hardware asserts the corresponding
- OC_ALERT signal to engage throttling. Throttling remains engaged for a preprogrammed
amount of time after the over-current signal deasserts. Optionally, software can be interrupted on the event. This is implemented using a simple timer which controls the assertion of the related OC_ALERT signal that goes to SOC_THERM_throttlectl block.
- Software Setup: Software needs to set up two things: how much to throttle and how long
to throttle beyond the OC-event deassertion. Then once armed, software need not do anything else. The purpose is to slow the system down for a period of time to cool things off before returning to normal operation.
- Software Response to Over-current Event: Once armed, software doesn’t need to do
anything. Software can be optionally interrupted after a certain number of these events have occurred. Repeated occurrences may indicate that software needs to switch to a more conservative frequency capping policy. - - - 2.
- Configuration Registers: Software is responsible for configuring the behavior of each OC
alarm. SOC_THERM itself does not have any hardware mechanism to distinguish between the different OC alarms.
- Interrupt Control/Enable/Disable/Status Registers: Each OC alarm is associated with an
interrupt threshold register and software is interrupted when this threshold is breached (if its set to 0, interrupt is disabled, set to 1 means software is interrupted on every event). 3.
##### 9.8.1.3.2 Throttling Hardware Requirements
The over-current alarm signal is routed to the Thermal Throttling Controller (SOC_THERM).
- Depending on the OC alarm configuration, SOC_THERM_edp controls the corresponding OC_ALERT
indications to the SOC_THERM_throttlectl block.
- In brief throttling mode, a programmable timer (THROTTLE_PERIOD) is needed to control the
corresponding OC_ALERT signal. The timer is enabled when the external OC alarm is deasserted. The OC_ALERT to SOC_THERM_throttlectl is asserted until the timer expires. In sticky throttling mode, the OC_ALERT to SOC_THERM_throttlectl block remains asserted until software can respond.
#### 9.8.1.4 Throttling Controller (SOC_THERM_throttlectl)
Clock slowdown is the primary response method for Thermal and EDP management.
- SOC_THERM_throttlectl performs the mapping of thermal and electrical events to different
throttling clock throttling levels. CPU and GPU are the two clocks which receive such throttle indicators. The outputs of the throttlectl block are separate 3-bit throttle indicators to the CPU and GPU. These three bits represent different throttling levels in a thermometer style to prevent glitches. 3’b000: No throttling (Default)

- Thermal Registers
3’b001: Light throttling 3’b011: Medium throttling 3’b111: Heavy throttling It is the decision of the respective clock domains in terms of what to do with the signals. For example, the respective clock domains will need to make sure to update their clocks in a safe manner. Ultimately, they decide how to go from light/medium/heavy to an actual frequency value.
#### 9.8.1.5 FSI_THERM
- There is another instance of SOC_THERM located in the Functional Safety Island named
FSI_THERM. FSI_THERM aids in the thermal management of Functional Safety Island (FSI). It processes data from the thermal sensors placed within FSI and signals events based on software configuration. These events are used by software to take appropriate actions (i.e., reset by PMC, Error Handling by Safety CPU) based on the nature of the event generated. FSI houses two thermal sense macros. FSI_THERM compares the two sensors for consistency and notifies an error if the difference between the sensors is beyond the programmed temperature range.
### 9.8.2 Thermal Registers
Refer to "Reading Register Tables" in the Introduction chapter for the register table protocol as well as recommendations for accessing registers. All SOC_THERM registers are in the APB address space. Refer to the Address Map section for the base addresses.
