- Scratch Registers
#### 9.10.3.2 Scratch Registers
For base address of this block of registers, please refer to SCRATCH in the System Address Map. These scratch registers are implemented in SRAM. Hence, the reset values below may not always hold true such that they all need initialization.
- SCRATCH_SECURE_RSV67_SCRATCH_0
- Offset: 0x27c,0x280
- Read/Write: R/W
- Parity Protection: N
- SCR Protection: SCRATCH_SCR_RSV67_SCR_0
- Reset: 0x00000000 (0b0000,0000,0000,0000,0000,0000,0000,0000)
- Bit
- Reset
- Description
31:0 0x0
- DATA
## 9.11 System Registers
### 9.11.1 Miscellaneous Registers
This register provides the chip ID and revision information. Chip revision information can be used when software needs to identify the revision when behavior is different between revisions. Refer to the datasheet or errata document to see if this applies.
- MISCREG_HIDREV_0
- Offset: 0x4
- Read/Write: RO
- Parity Protection: N
- Shadow: N
- SCR Protection: 0
- Reset: 0x00012347 (0bxxxx,xxx0,0000,0001,0010,0011,0100,0111)
- Bit
- Reset
- Description
24:20 0x0 PRE_SI_PLATFORM:
- Set to 0 on silicon platform; set to pre-silicon platform type on pre-
silicon platforms. 0 = SILICON 1-9 = reserved for pre-silicon use

- Hardware Performance Monitor (HWPM)
- Bit
- Reset
- Description
19:16 0x1 MINORREV: The encoding of this field is as below. A,B,C,D denote major revs, while 01, 02, 03 denote minor revision. 0000:Reserved 0001:A01 0010:A02 0011:A03 0100:Reserved 0101:B01 0110:B02 0111:B03 1000:Reserved 1001:C01 1010:C02 1011:C03 1100:Reserved 1101:D01 1110:D02 1111:D03 15:8 0x23 CHIPID: Upper two digits of the SoC ID. 7:4 0x4 MAJORREV: This field provides the last digit of the Chip ID. 3:0 0x7 HIDFAM: Chip ID family register. 0-6 = reserved for other NVIDIA products 7 = APXX_TXX (NVIDIA SOC)
## 9.12 Hardware Performance Monitor (HWPM)
### 9.12.1 Overview
The Hardware Performance Monitor is useful for performance monitoring. Key features include:
- Periodic sampling, in the range 1 Hz to 1 MHz, of performance counters without CPU polling
or high CPU overhead. Uniform approach to capture SoC IP performance metrics for SoC clients. A programmable approach to capturing performance events.
- GPU performance monitors support a variety of modes for capturing performance
statistics. Ability to capture SoC, CPU, and GPU bandwidth utilization in a single pass. Provides a performance debug platform for graphics and visualization software developers.
- The SoC has a fully separate performance monitor subsystem from the hardware performance
monitor (HWPM) subsystem of the GPU. This includes a separate programming interface and

- Hardware Performance Monitor (HWPM)
separate record streaming path to memory. Detailed information on the system level diagram of the HWPM subsystem can be found in the System Block Diagram section below. Available performance monitoring systems include:
- GPU HWPMs
- Profiling of GPU performance metrics
-
- Both period and range-based profiling
- Record streaming to memory and register polling support
- Context save/restore support
- SOC HWPMs
- Profiling of SoC IP and memory subsystem metrics
- Record streaming to memory and register polling support
- No context save/restore support
- MC stats
- Counters to capture bandwidth statistics
- Register polling support only
- Client and address filtering
The following IP blocks are affected by the features described in this chapter:
- HWPM blocks
- PERFMUX, PERFMON, HUB, ROUTER, PMA
- VI
- ISP
- VIC
- OFA
- PVA
- NVDLA
- MGBE
- PCIe
- SCF
- NVENC
- NVDEC
- DISPLAY
- MSS
The following software stacks are affected by features described in this chapter:
- SoC HWPM driver
- DEVTOOLS: product level performance monitor toolset
- Hardware infrastructure tools: Internal performance monitor toolset

- Hardware Performance Monitor (HWPM)
- BPMP driver
- Table 9.92 Glossary
- Abbreviation/Term
- Definition
- APB
- Advanced Peripheral Bus
- CBB
- Control Backbone
- DLA
- Deep Learning Accelerator
- GPU
- Graphics Processing Unit
- PERFMON
- Performance Monitor (unit)
- HWPM
- Hardware Performance Monitor (full path)
- PMA
- Performance Monitor Accumulator
- PVA
- Programmable Vision Accelerator
- RPG
- Remote Perfmon (performance monitor) Group
- VPM
- Virtual Performance Monitor
- VM
- Virtual Machine
#### 9.12.1.1 System Block Diagram
- The HWPM path includes a performance mux (PERFMUX) that funnels the performance monitoring
events out of the SoC IP, a performance monitor (PERFMON) used for counting the selected output events, HUBs that funnel performance monitor count outputs for a given local group of
- PERFMONs, a SYS ROUTER that captures results from all the HUBs and, finally a performance
monitor accumulator (PMA). The figure below illustrates the HWPM paths in both the SoC and the GPU. A local PERFMON cluster and HUB is instanced as a single block called a remote perfmon group (RPG). Ideally, each PERFMON runs on the same clock as that used to generate the event.
- However, HWPMs do allow for asynchronous event capture. For this, events must be generated
from a clock of equal or lower frequency to the PERFMON clock. The clock used to capture and forward RPG outputs to SYS ROUTER and PMA is common across all SoC IPs.

- Hardware Performance Monitor (HWPM)
**Figure 9.91 HWPM Block Diagram**
- Programming of the SoC HWPM path is performed by a common SoC HWPM driver. HWPM
registers exist outside of the IP. Enables and selects for PERFMUXs are captured in the IP using local IP registers. A separate aperture exists for programming the RPG PERFMONs. Because the
- HWPM design originates from GPU, the programming interface for PERFMON blocks is PRI. Each
RPG instances an APB2PRI shim to allow the RPG to connect to the SoC control backbone (CBB). Note there is no change to programming of GPU HWPM path from previous generations.
##### 9.12.1.1.1 PERFMON Record Streaming
The ability to stream performance metrics through a side channel path while the application is running provides minimally invasive techniques for gathering performance statistics. SoC and GPU
- HWPM subsystems allow for PERFMON record streaming. Snapshots of PERFMON records can be
timer based (frequency-based profiling) or event based (range-based profiling). Frequency-based profiling relies on a programmable count down value. After the timer reaches zero, all PERFMON records are snapped and streamed to memory. Range-based profiling relies on engine triggers from IPs containing PERFMONs to snap and stream all PERFMONs in the SoC to memory. Profiling tools use these triggers to denote start and stop of a given engine context. Accurate performance information is gained by isolating the engine context.

- Hardware Performance Monitor (HWPM)
- Records streamed from PERFMONs can be gathered from 16 or 32-bit counters. 32-byte records
are streamed out from each PERFMON once it is triggered. Events are captured at the speed of the IP clock driving the event and can be sent back-to-back. The figure below and the IPs Utilizing HWPMs section provide more details on the HWPM record streaming path and IP PERFMON count to help understand performance metric capture bandwidth implications based on capture granularity.
**Figure 9.92 Record Streaming Path**
Bandwidth of the capture stream is application dependent. An example of PERFMON record stream bandwidth is provided below:
- PERFMONs are set to Mode C (using 16-bit counters)
- Counter threshold = 0xF000 (~60,000)
- Maximum IP event clock = 1.2GHz
64 PERFMONs in SoC HWPM subsystems
- Threshold trigger period
60,000/1.2GHz = 50us
- Data capture size
32B record * 64 PERFMONs = 2KB
- Sustained bandwidth
2KB/50us = 41MB/s
- Burst bandwidth
16B*625MHz = 10GB/s for 200ns

- Hardware Performance Monitor (HWPM)
- To support 1MHz sample rate for all PERFMONs requires roughly 2GB/s of sustained memory
bandwidth. The HWPM memory bandwidth is capped at 1GB, allowing up to half of the PERFMONs to be sampled at 1MHz or all PERFMONs to be sampled at 500KHz rate or every 2us. PMA records are stored in a circular buffer in memory. The figure below describes how records are accessed in the buffer whose location and size are set using PMA OUTBASE and OUTSIZE.
- Hardware increments MEM_HEAD after every record store. Software maintains the read pointer
and indicates read pointer increments via updates to PMA MEM_BUMP.
**Figure 9.93 PMA Circular Buffer**
#### 9.12.1.2 Performance Monitor Description
A key advantage of the HWPM is their programmable nature. Based on the PERFMON mode, events can be used as single bit increment pulse, or a combination of signals can be used to increment a count by a programmable amount. It is possible to define an experiment using any combination of events going to the PERFMON. The experiment defines the level required for all events and the amount to increment the counter based on the event levels. The table below illustrates examples of experiments for the available PERFMON modes. Additionally, timestamps can be written out along with the count. Note that mode C counter width is only 16 bits when supporting 12 counters. To avoid saturation of the counter, PERFMON snaps and streams out an intermediate record when any counter reaches the thresholds in the table below. Tools can detect overflow records and aggregate into a wider software counter. If a PERFMON has back-pressure and cannot snap a record, then the counter saturates at the maximum value.
- Table 9.93 Performance Monitor Streaming Overflow Threshold
- Counter Width
- Threshold
16-bit 0xF000

- Hardware Performance Monitor (HWPM)
- Counter Width
- Threshold
32-bit 0xF0000000
- Table 9.94 Performance Monitor Mode Examples
- Mode
- Maximum
- Counters
- Counter Width
- Increment
- Streaming
A 1 with state 1 or “const value”
- No
- B1
1 to 12 bits
- No
C 1 or “const value”
- Yes
C 12x16b or [4 x 16b, 4 x 32b] 1 or “const value”
- Yes
C with local trigger 10 x 16b 1 or “const value”
- Yes
- E2
1 to 12 bits
- Yes
1. 2.
- Mode B assumes General Counting Mode (GCM) is used
- Mode E assumes General Counting Mode (GCM) is used
#### 9.12.1.3 Performance Event Considerations
It is useful to consider performance in terms of the metrics germane to the IP being monitored. For example, OFA IP might consider “motion vectors per second”. This requires two pieces of information: motion vector count and timestamps associated with the count. Other metrics may include two or more counts.
##### 9.12.1.3.1 Average Latency Support
- The SoC HWPM supports latency histograms within the PERFMONs. However, this requires the
entire PERFMON, thus limiting performance metric capture to a single metric. By supporting average latency, a fraction of the Mode C PERFMON counters are available for capturing other metrics. A key metric to capture with latency is the associated IP bandwidth. Average latency requires three counters:
- Request Count (R_COUNT)
- Accumulated over the profiling period
- Outstanding Request Count (OR_COUNT)
- Increments with request
- Decrements with response
- Count changes every cycle based on requests and response activity
- Count returns to zero when all responses are received

- Hardware Performance Monitor (HWPM)
- Accumulated Outstanding Request Count (AOR_COUNT)
- Accumulates the outstanding request count over time
- AOR_COUNT = AOR_COUNT + OR_COUNT
The average latency for an outstanding request is calculated using the following formula:
- Average Latency = AOR_COUNT / R_COUNT
The figure below illustrates an example of calculating average latency using the three counters listed above where two back-to-back requests are followed by back-to-back responses seven cycles later.
**Figure 9.94 Average Latency Example**
Support for average latency with HWPM requires IP to implement the outstanding request count (OR_COUNT) prior to the PERFMUX. Additionally, the PERFMON group must contain the following inputs:
- Request Count
- Outstanding Request Count
Resetting of outstanding request count prior to profiling run helps ensure a more accurate count of outstanding requests per cycle. To avoid counter wrapping negative, IPs should clamp the outstanding request count (OR_COUNT) to zero.
- Accumulated Outstanding Request Support
- AOR_COUNT is implemented with PERFMON counters. Two implementations are described in this
section:
- Using multiple PERFMON counters
- Allows for capturing outstanding requests at a finer granularity

- Hardware Performance Monitor (HWPM)
- Useful for high frequency sampling of PERFMON records of high bandwidth clients
- Using single PERFMON counter
- Allows for capturing over six additional metrics in a single pass
- Useful for capturing average latency over wider time periods
#### 9.12.1.4 Performance Trigger Considerations
Performance monitors use a variety of triggers to cause PERFMON to output data to memory. Reasons to output performance monitor records are:
- Periodic sampling
- Simultaneously snapping all IP PERFMONS. PERFMON is configured to snap on
pma_trigger. pma_trigger is driven by a periodic pulse from the PMA.
- Performance monitor overflow
- PERFMON automatically writes out a record if the count reaches a fixed threshold. See the
“Performance Monitor Streaming Overflow Threshold” table.
- IP context switching
- Forcing records to memory for a given IP when IP switches contexts
- Required for multi-VM platforms
- Local trigger
- Cross triggering
- Triggering record writes between SoC and GPU HWPM subsystems
- CPU trigger
- CPU generated trigger based upon PMA register write
- NV_PERF_PMA_TRIGGER_GLOBAL (used by many tools)
- NV_PERF_PMA_SYS_TRIGGER_START
- NV_PERF_PMA_SYS_TRIGGER_STOP
- - -
- CPU generated local trigger based upon PMM register write
- -
- NV_PERF_PMM_CONTROLB_FLUSH_RECORD
- NV_PERF_PMM_STARTEXPERIMENT
- Local PERFMON trigger
-
- PERFMONs can be configured to snap records on other events on the PERFMON watch
bus. t_pulse – Local periodic clock with rates of 2{10-16} cycles in the PERFMONs clock domain.
- Mode E – any 1-bit signal (on a pulse)
- Mode C – any 4-bit predicate (including t_pulse, pma_trigger, etc.)
valid + 32-bit bookmark – direct IP trigger to cause snapshot

- Hardware Performance Monitor (HWPM)
In general, the SoC HWPMs configures PERFMONS to snap based upon periodic triggers from the
- PMA. The HWPM subsystem accomplishes periodic sampling by implementing a count-down
trigger in PMA. Once the counter reaches zero, PMA triggers all PERFMONs to snap the counters and stream a 32-byte record to the PMA to be written to memory. SoC HWPMs support triggering for all the reasons listed except for cross triggering between SoC IPs. Cross triggering is only allowed between SoC and GPU HWPM subsystems (PMAs).
##### 9.12.1.4.1 Performance Metric Record Alignment
Performance metric records contain up to a 40-bit “timestamp”. This timestamp is measured in the clock domain of the local PERFMON. This can only be correlated to wall clock time if the frequency of the PERFMON clock domain is fixed. The PMA unit has a feature called PMA record that outputs a global wall clock timestamp (TSC) as part of its record along with TOTAL_TRIG_COUNT that can be matched to PERFMON record TOTAL_TRIG_RCV count to correlate to wall clock time. The following sequence illustrates how the correlation between PERFMON free running counter timestamp and PMA (TSC) timestamp is established:
- PMA periodic timer pops
- PMA sends global trigger to all PERFMONs
- PMA outputs TSC TIMESTAMP and TOTAL_TRIG_COUNT
- PERFMONs send records to PMA with “local counter” TIMESTAMP and TOTAL_TRIG_RCV
- Post processing looks for PERFMON records where TOTAL_TRIG_COUNT equals
- TOTAL_TRIG_RCV. The relationship between wall clock and local timer is established using
corresponding PMA and PERFMON TIMESTAMPs. When considering local triggers, records of a given IP are injected into the HWPM record buffer between the sampled records. Local triggered records can be identified by the
- PMLOCALTRIG_COUNTA field > 0. Tools can either use linear interpolation of PERFMON record
timestamps using two neighboring PMA trigger records or rely on bookmarks to align performance metric records to the IP VM timestamp records in a separate buffer. Correlation of locally triggered and periodically sampled records can be achieved using timestamps in these record types.
##### 9.12.1.4.2 PERFMON Local Triggers
PERFMON supports a variety of local triggers. Local trigger usage is available in Mode C and Mode E specifically external bookmark trigger. A description of local trigger is as follows:
- Modes C and E supports PERFMON Local Triggers. This is a hardwired interface. Each local
trigger supports: 1-bit trigger wire 16-bit bookmark IP implementing local triggers outputs two local trigger/bookmarks:

- Hardware Performance Monitor (HWPM)
- PMA_ENGINE_START, PM_LOCAL_TRIGA, PM_LOCAL_BKMKA
- PMA_ENGINE_STOP, PM_LOCAL_TRIGB, PM_LOCAL_BKMKB
- TOTAL_TRIG_RCV and PM_LOCAL_TRIGB share the same location in the 32-byte record. IP
should use PM_LOCAL_TRIGA.
- Trigger outputs are from IP local trigger registers
IP microprocessor (Falcon or Cortex-R5):
- Writes bookmark
- Toggles trigger
- PERFMON snaps (write out record) when local trigger wire is asserted. If the PERFMON is
unable to snap due to backpressure, the record may be merged with additional PMA or local triggers. If two local triggers are merged, the local trigger count is two and the bookmark is from the most recent trigger. A tool can identify this case and still correlate to the two events in the IPs event log. It is recommended that bookmarks are implemented as a monotonically incrementing ID to support this case. Output PERFMON record contains:
- Mode C
-
- Single 16-bit bookmark and local trigger count
- Mode E
-
- Two 16-bit bookmarks and two local trigger counts
- Mode C Record
15 8 7 0 .--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+-----. 0x00 | timestamp[15:0] | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ 0x02 | TOTAL_TRIG_RCV[7:0] | timestamp[23:16] | <-- or TOTAL_TRIG_RCV[7:0] only if
- TRIGRCV_IN_MODEC is set
+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ 0x04 | PERFMONID[7:0] | TOTAL_TRIG_RCV[15:8] | <-- or TOTAL_TRIG_RCV[15:8] only if TRIGRCV_IN_MODEC is set +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ 0x06 |0 |0 |0 |DS|PMID[10:8]| SMPLCNT[8:0] | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ 0x08 | count0[15:0] | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+

- Hardware Performance Monitor (HWPM)
0x0A | count1[15:0] | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ 0x0C | count2[15:0] | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ 0x0E | count3[15:0] | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ 0x10 | count4[15:0] | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ 0x12 | count5[15:0] | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ 0x14 | count6[15:0] | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ 0x16 | count7[15:0] | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ 0x18 | count8[15:0] | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ 0x1A | count9[15:0] | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ 0x1C | pm local trigger A count | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ 0x1E | bookmark A | `--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+-----'
- Mode E Record
15 8 7 0 .--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+-----. 0x00 | timestamp[15:0] | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+

- Hardware Performance Monitor (HWPM)
0x02 | timestamp[31:16] | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ 0x04 | PERFMONID[7:0] | timestamp[39:32] | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ 0x06 |0 |0 |0 |DS|PMID[10:8]| SMPLCNT[8:0] | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ 0x08 | count0[15:0] | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ 0x0A | count0[31:16] | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ 0x0C | count1[15:0] | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ 0x0E | count1[31:16] | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ 0x10 | count2[15:0] | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ 0x12 | count2[31:16] | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ 0x14 | count3[15:0] | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ 0x16 | count3[31:16] | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ 0x18 | pm local trigger B count | <-- or TOTAL_TRIG_RCV[15:0] if +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ PMLOCALTRIGB_EN_DISABLE 0x1A | bookmark B | <-- or TOTAL_TRIG_RCV[31:0] if +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ PMLOCALTRIGB_EN_DISABLE 0x1C | pm local trigger A count | +--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+ 0x1E | bookmark A |

- Hardware Performance Monitor (HWPM)
`--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+--+-----' Use of local triggers to support VM annotation is described in the Virtualization Support section.
- While other local triggers mechanisms are available in HWPM, triggers occur via access to
PERFMON registers. Access to PERFMON registers from IP microcontroller is not supported. Use of local triggers are limited to IPs running on platforms using a variety VMs over a given period. The following IPs require local triggering:
- VI
- ISP
- PVA
- VIC
- OFA
- NVENC
- NVDLA
##### 9.12.1.4.3 GPU-SOC Cross Triggers
- HWPMs can capture or “snap” PERFMON records globally in memory via trigger signals. These
triggers are local to the performance monitor accumulator (PMA) and described later in this section. There are two separate HWPM subsystems in the SoC. These two subsystems utilize cross triggers for handshaking between the GPU and SoC. This feature benefits two types of tools: 1.
- Periodic Sampling
a. b.
- Enable consistent sampling period
- Improve low frequency sampling avoiding skew and drift between PERFMON systems
- Support low overhead synchronized event-based sampling
c. i.
- Example: system task manager triggers at start and end of each task
2.
- Range Profiling
a. b.
- Allow for asynchronous trigger via GPU command buffer
- Critical for graphics/compute triage of memory system counter
i.
- GPU HWPM path has very poor memory observation at both HSHUB and MSS
To accomplish the required handshake, one HWPM path is the initiator (generate triggers from PMA) and the other HWPM path is the target (receive triggers to PMA). The initiator generates a packet-based trigger where:
- Trigger start packet indicates start capturing performance monitor events in memory
- Trigger stop packet indicates stop capturing performance monitor events in memory

- Hardware Performance Monitor (HWPM)
Target HWPM path suspends capture of performance monitor events unless trigger is active when it is configured to wait on a trigger. Either SoC or GPU PMA can act as an initiator and cause a trigger to the other PMA. The trigger is accomplished via a write to PMA’s programmable global output trigger register. These bits are directly output from PMA as engine triggers and typically connect to PERFMONs in the HWPM subsystem to generate a global trigger. PMA also supports input triggers to capture records. In GPU, these triggers typically connect to engines utilizing performance monitors to allow multi- engine monitoring controlled by a single engine. One GPU PMA global output trigger is connected to an SoC PMA input engine trigger. Also, one SoC PMA global output trigger is connected to a GPU PMA input engine trigger. Together, these triggers enable cross triggering between HWPM subsystems in both directions. Due to clock synchronization issues on cross trigger input to GPU, there is a potential for metastability within GPU PMA on the engine trigger input path. Therefore, SoC to GPU cross triggering is not available in the SoC. However, GPU to SoC cross triggering is supported and there are no metastability issues in this direction.
- SoC to GPU cross triggering requires triggers be spaced a minimum of 35 LA clock cycles
(625MHz) to avoid potential corruption to trigger counts received.
##### 9.12.1.4.4 SoC PMA Engine Triggers
The HWPM PMA supports up to 64 engine triggers. Each engine can support a 1-bit start, a 1-bit stop, and a 32-bit bookmark. The start and stop signals are passed to the PMA trigger state machine. An engine should pulse the start or stop for one cycle.
- Table 9.95 SoC PMA Engine Triggers
- ENGINE
- START
- STOP
- BOOKMARK
- PMA
- NV_PERF_GLOBAL_TRIGGER_START
- NV_PERF_GLOBAL_TRIGGER_
- START
- Not supported
- GPU-PMA-SYS
- GPU-PMA-SYS-PMA-TRIGGER level
signal passed through clock domain crossing and edge to pulse generator. 0 (tied to ground)
- Not supported
- VI
- PMA_ENGINE_START
- PMA_ENGINE_STOP
- Not supported
- ISP
- PMA_ENGINE_START
- PMA_ENGINE_STOP
- Not Supported
- PVA
- PMA_ENGINE_START
- PMA_ENGINE_STOP
- Not Supported
- VIC
- PMA_ENGINE_START
- PMA_ENGINE_STOP
- Not Supported
- OFA
- PMA_ENGINE_START
- PMA_ENGINE_STOP
- Not Supported
- NVDEC
- PMA_ENGINE_START
- PMA_ENGINE_STOP
- Not Supported
- NVDLA
- PMA_ENGINE_START
- PMA_ENGINE_STOP
- Not Supported

- Hardware Performance Monitor (HWPM)
- ENGINE
- START
- STOP
- BOOKMARK
- MGBE
- PMA_ENGINE_START
- PMA_ENGINE_STOP
- Not Supported
- PCIe
- PMA_ENGINE_START
- PMA_ENGINE_STOP
- Not Supported
- DISPLAY

- PMA_ENGINE_STOP
- Not Supported
Triggers sourced at the IP level are necessary to support range-based profiling. This requires one or both of the following:
- The ability for the IP microprocessor to generate local triggers
Support via Local Trigger registers start and stop trigger bits as described in the PERFMON
- Local Triggers
- Only saves IP PERFMON records
- Predefined hardware events used to generate engine triggers
- Events may have element of programmability via IP register configuration bits. Events can
be qualified with: - - -
- Trigger arming
- Mode qualifying
- Trigger count
- Saves records of all PERFMONs in the SoC
The PMA trigger system supports per engine start and stop masks to disable specific engines. All engine triggers must be aligned to LA clock and are single active high pulse. Note that for use cases where engine triggers are used in the SoC, it is possible to lose a trigger due to incorrect sizing of split FIFO in the engine trigger path to PMA.
#### 9.12.1.5 Memory Subsystem Single Pass Profiling
- The MSS HWPM path supports single pass performance monitoring where memory bandwidth
statistics are simultaneously collected in three categories:
- CPU traffic
- GPU traffic
- Total traffic
These three categories are not programmable. To implement this capability, MSS contains HWPMs over all memory channels. Using client ID (CGID), associated CPU, GPU and total traffic counters in the channel are incremented appropriately. Each client ID supports the following four counters:
- Bytes read
- Bytes write

- Hardware Performance Monitor (HWPM)
- Cycles read active
- Cycle write active
Mode C is required to collect all three clients. Either bytes or utilization can be collected from any two clients using Mode E. When collecting this data, it is critical to understand observation enabled by Mode C versus counter width of Mode E. If PERFMONs operate at 1GHz and there are 16
- PERFMONS, then Mode C generates 32 bytes/record x channels x 1GHz / 0xF000 = ~8MB of data
per second, whereas Mode E generates 512 bytes. SoC HWPM can be configured to collect this information via periodic PMA triggers.
- GPU HWPM has access to MSS via wires from MSS to the GPU HWPM mc0 PERFMON, which
requires many two passes x channels for bytes or utilization. This is also supported for GPU tools that support both periodic sampling (PerfInspector, Nsight GPU Trace) and range profiling (Cyclestats, Perfalyze, Nsight Graphics Range Profiler, Nsight Compute Profiler). Periodic sampling generally uses PMA periodic triggers. Range profiling uses asynchronous push buffer methods to generate PMA triggers around a range of GPU work. GPU range profiling can be performed at a GPU device level or for a GPU graphics engine context. In graphics engine context level profiling, the GPU Front End Context Switch (FECS) unit is responsible for freezing the HWPM PM system, draining in flight records, saving the state of the PERFMONS and PMA, and later restoring the state of the PERFMONS and PMA. A GPU range profiler must use both the SoC HWPM and GPU HWPM system to capture MSS counters within the specified asynchronous GPU range. The following sequence describes GPU device level range profiling:
- Reserve HWPM
1.
- Begin Session
a. b.
- Reserve SoC HWPM Device Level
- Reserve GPU HWPM Device Level
- Disable GPU Power Management
[OPTIONAL] Lock GPU Clocks
- Setup GPU HWPM
c. d. e. i.
- Allocate PMA Buffer
- Map PMA Buffer
- Reset PMA
- Set Trigger Mode to Mixed Mode or Fermi Mode
- Bind PMA Buffer
- Reset Trigger Counts
ii. iii. iv. v. vi. f.
- Setup SoC HWPM
i.
- Allocate PMA Buffer

- Hardware Performance Monitor (HWPM)
ii.
- Map PMA Buffer
- Reset PMA
- Set Trigger Mode to Tesla
- Set Engine Trigger Mask to GPU PMA Engine
- Configure SoC PMA as a cross trigger target. Set PMA to trigger on GPU PMA engine
start triggers. Set the GPU engine for Tesla trigger mode (toggle). This propagates all triggers from GPU PMA SYS pma_trigger to SoC PMA SYS pma_triggers.
- Reset Trigger Counts
iii. iv. v. vi. vii. 2.
- Begin Pass
a.
- GPU
i. Convert PERFMON and perfmux regops to push buffer and/or push methods. b.
- SoC
i.
- Convert PERFMON and perfmux regops to SoC HWPM driver format and execute a
register operation SYSCALL to the SoC HWPM driver to configure units (MC). The tool may choose to keep this configuration for all passes. c.
- Begin Range
i.
- GPU - Push PmTrigger (results in start trigger from graphics engine)
Push any other graphics works. ii. d.
- End Range
i.
- GPU – Push WaitForIdle
GPU – Push PmTrigger (results in start trigger from graphics engine). It is also possible to use end of pipe trigger, but due to need to guarantee isolation and no merged triggers, there is usually a wait for idle and delay between ranges.
- GPU – Push DELAY to ensure EndRange trigger and the next BeginRange trigger does not
merge due to back pressure. Not all PERFMON systems have enough buffering for 2-3 back-to-back triggers. ii. iii. e.
- Repeat (c) Begin Range – (d) End Range
- End Pass
f. i.
- GPU – Push Wait for Idle
This is the end of the frame of work. In the case of APIC based replay or kernel replay, the state of the system is restored to before the frame.
- Process PMA Buffer
ii. iii. a.
- For SoC PMA and GPU PMA
i. This can be done using a CPU/GPU synchronization or from a background thread.
- For GPU use case
ii. a. b.
- CPU PRI write to flush, and CPU PRI read to get buffer
- CPU poll on PRI read value equal memory value
- Process buffer
- CPU PRI write to update PMA buffer bytes processed
c. d.

- Hardware Performance Monitor (HWPM)
e.
- GPU can be optimized to perform the PRI operations through the push buffer to
avoid SYSCALL overhead. Across multiple frames the PRI operations can be batched to avoid number of SYSCALLs. iii.
- For CPU use case, the same operations are performed through the SoC HWPM
driver. g. h.
- Repeat (b) Begin Pass – (f) End Pass
- End Session
i. Clean-up all operations performed in Begin Session plus all PERFMONS and PERFMUXES. The following sequence describes GPU Context Switched Range Profiling:
- Same as Device Level but Reservation of GPU HWPM is at GPU context level and GPU HWPM
context switch is enabled.
- If a GPU context switch occurs in the middle of a range, then tools extend GPU FECS µcode
to execute a GPU PMA PRI trigger before save and after restore of the target graphics engine context. This generates additional records. The table below shows the PMA records output. Pass 1 has no GPU graphics engine context switch. Pass 2 has a context switch in the middle of range 2. For handling context switching assume that End Range (ii) above will pushes WaitForIdle and PmTrigger (start trigger) for the end of a range. The tool does not push a PmTriggerEnd (to generate a graphics engine end of pipe trigger).

determine if GPU context switches occurred during a range or between ranges. In the case of Pass 2 Range 1, a context switch occurred so the tool processes both CPU and GPU records using the TOTAL TRIGGER COUNT == TOTAL_TRIG_RCV as:
- Pass 2 Range 1 Values

#### 9.12.1.6 Programming Path
##### 9.12.1.6.1 Register Space
Programming of SoC HWPM path registers is accomplished via a common SoC HWPM driver. Registers reside in HWPM blocks outside of the IP. There are four HWPM registers groups:
- PERFMUX (part of IP register space)
- PERFMON
- SYS ROUTER
- PMA
The following table provides information on register group control parameters and register space size. The performance monitor registers lie in a contiguous space due to GPU build script limitations.
- Table 9.96 Performance Monitor Registers
- Information
- Register Space
- PERFMUX
- PERFMON
- SYS ROUTER
- PMA
- Control Functions
- Mux Enable, Selects
- Monitor experiment
parameters
- PERFMON record
streaming control
- Buffer Control,
- Triggers
- Size per space
4B per register 4KB 512B 8KB
- Total Size over IPs
N/A 2MB 4KB 8KB
- Contiguous over IPs
- No
- Yes
- Yes
- Yes
- Programming of the SoC HWPM path occurs prior to starting the use case and collection of
performance statistics. Reprogramming of HWPM registers during the use case is not supported.
##### 9.12.1.6.2 Power Sequencing
Power sequencing for SoC IPs containing HWPMs makes the following assumptions:
- IP containing HWPMs but not involved in profiling may power up or down during performance
monitoring
- IP and HWPM RPG reside in the same physical chiplet
- RPG final output is via split FIFO (supporting CDC and VDC)
- RPG supports IP reset (via IP output clamp) and global HWPM reset (LA clock aligned)

- Hardware Performance Monitor (HWPM)
The power up/power down sequences below should be followed by all IPs utilizing HWPMs.
- Power up sequence
- For IPs using ASYNC reset
- - - -
- Assert IP reset
- Ungate IP power
- Ungate IP clocks
- Unclamp IP outputs
- For IPs using SYNC reset
- - - -
- Ungate IP power
- Ungate IP clocks
- Assert IP reset
- Unclamp IP outputs
- Power down sequence
- Ensure IP is idle
- Ensure IP performance monitoring is off
- Ensure PERFMON counters are zero
- Reset IP
- Clamp IP outputs
- Gate IP clocks
- Gate IP voltage
- Remove IP reset
Note that RPG logic uses only ASYNC reset flops. RPGs utilize IP power clamps instead of IP resets for resetting RPG flops. This ensures that clamps are always active when clocks start, regardless of IP reset levels.
#### 9.12.1.7 Virtualization Support
- HWPM virtualization support annotates the accelerator’s performance monitor metrics with
information on virtual machine (VM) occupancy of the accelerator over time, as illustrated in the figure below.
**Figure 9.95 IP VM Switch Example**

- Hardware Performance Monitor (HWPM)
The timeline in the figure shows periodic sampling (PMA_trigger) of an accelerator’s performance counter (accelerator_cntr) and the VM context active on the accelerator (accelerator_vm). The last row (with local_trigger) shows an improved version of the performance counter collection, where an additional global or local trigger occurs on an accelerator context switch, so performance counters can correctly be attributed to a context. Virtual OS support is accomplished via post processing of HWPM records with timestamp and VM switching information from hardware accelerators (IPs). HWPM virtualization support makes the following assumptions:
- Memory buffer exists per IP used to store timestamp, bookmark, and VM switch information
- Buffer read/write accessible by IP firmware and read accessible by SoC HWPM driver
Local trigger support exists in IP to cause writes of IP performance metric record to memory
- IP contains a microprocessor (Falcon or Cortex-R5) and has access to global timer (TSC)
The following steps outline the IP sequence to support VM annotation to PERFMON records:
- IP completes processing of context A
IP signals a local trigger to PERFMONs. Generate trigger event via:
- IP microprocessor (Falcon or Cortex-R5) write to “local trigger register”
- -
- Sets bookmark
- Toggles trigger
- PERFMON record snapped to memory via local trigger
- See the Performance Trigger Considerations section for local trigger description
- IP microprocessor (Falcon or Cortex-R5) reads TSC
- IP microprocessor determines next VM context to run
IP writes a record to “IP timestamp buffer” with the following information:
- Timestamp (TSC value)
If the event is a switch event, then the event contains an identifier for the old VM context and an identifier for the new VM context.
- If the event is a range event, then start and end events contain an identifier for the VM
context. This generates 2x the number of events as a switch event. 16-bit bookmark value that corresponds to local trigger bookmark.
- IP starts processing context B
Using the context switch information in the IP VM switch buffer, it is possible to annotate performance metrics with “current VM” information by aligning performance record metric bookmarks with those in the IP VM switch buffer. Local triggers enable performance metric post processing to associate precise performance metric counts with a VM since the count is written to memory using the local trigger. Estimating the performance metric counts is possible by using the records of the periodic samples that happen before and after the timestamp associated with the VM switch by assigning counts using ratio of time differences between the timestamps of the two periodic samples and the VM switch

- Hardware Performance Monitor (HWPM)
timestamp. However, accuracy cannot be guaranteed since this approach assumes similar performance utilization between VMs.

#### 9.12.2.1 IPs Utilizing HWPMs

IP instance utilizing HWPMs instance PERFMUX(s) using a specification file build flow process. IPs implement PERFMUX select and enable via local registers using appropriate PERFMUX register template. Performance monitor events and grouping are described in the specification file. Event toggling frequency to PERFMUX must not exceed frequency of PERMON clock capturing PERFMUX output.
- IP Local Trigger Interface
IPs implementing local PERFMON triggers create Local Trigger registers whose fields are output to Remote Perfmon Group (RPG), as outlined in the Performance Trigger Considerations section. RPG connects trigger and bookmark as a common input to all PERFMONs in RPG. The following IPs implement local triggers:
- VI
- ISP
- PVA
- VIC
- OFA
- NVDEC
- NVENC
- NVDLA
IPs create a level trigger lasting for a minimum of three la_clk cycles. RPG samples local triggers A and B from IP using LA clock and create single active high pulse when inputs transition from zero to one.
- IP Engine Trigger Interface
- IPs implementing engine triggers create engine trigger registers and output register bits
- PMA_ENGINE_START and PMA_ENGINE_STOP to RPG, as outlined in the SoC PMA Engine Triggers
section. The following IPs implement engine triggers:
- VI
- ISP
- PVA
- VIC
- OFA
- NVDEC
- NVENC
- NVDLA
- MGBE
- PCIe
- DISPLAY

- Hardware Performance Monitor (HWPM)
- IPs create a level trigger lasting for a minimum of three la_clk cycles. RPG samples
- PMA_ENGINE_START and PMA_ENGINE_STOP from IP using LA clock and create single active high
pulse when inputs transition from zero to one.
##### 9.12.2.1.1 HWPM Blocks
- Remote PERFMON Group (RPG) Sub-blocks
RPGs are be comprised of the following units:
- HWPM PERFMON(s)
- HWPM HUB
- APB interface
- APB-to-PRIV shim (connect APB to PERFMON PRIV register block)
- Register block (one per PERFMON)
- Split FIFO output for clock and voltage domain crossing (CDC and VDC)
Trigger shim (covert positive edge level signal to single pulse for local and engine triggers)
- RPG Apertures
- RPG has single aperture group
- PERFMON: 4KB per PERFMON
PERFMON apertures must be contiguous within RPG and between RPGs.
- RPG Clocks, Resets and Power Gate Clamps
RPG uses up to three resets: pg_clamp_en for single power partition pg_clamp_en for multiple power partitions
- Referred to as kitchen sink pg_clamp_en
- Global HWPM reset
All RPG logic utilizes ASYNC reset flops.
- RPG reset instructions vary based on the RPG power partition configuration. The list below
describes the reset method for the different RPG power partition configuration. It is the responsibility of the RPG designer to understand which configuration applies to a given RPG.
- Single power partition
PERFMONs and HUB reset equation: -
- Global HWPM reset AND pg_clamp_en
PERFMON and HUB power gate:

- Hardware Performance Monitor (HWPM)
- pg_clamp_en
- Example: PVA
- Multiple power partitions with HUB power gated
PERFMON reset equation: -
- Global HWPM reset AND pg_clamp_en
HUB reset equation: -
- Global HWPM reset AND kitchen sink pg_clamp_en
PERFMON power gate: - pg_clamp_en HUB power gate: - kitchen sink pg_clamp_en
- Example: PCIe
- System Router Sub-blocks
SYSTEM ROUTER is comprised of the following units:
- HWPM SYS ROUTER
- APB interface
- APB-to-PRIV shim (connect APB to SYS ROUTER PRIV register blocks)
- Performance Monitor Accumulator (PMA) Sub-blocks
PMA is comprised of the following units:
- GPU HWPM PMA
- FBHUB-to-AXICIF shim
- APB interface
- APB-to-PRIV shim (connect APB to PMA PRIV register blocks)
- TSC-to-ptimer shim (converts serial-to-parallel data prior to GPU HWPM PMA)
- SYS Router and PMA Apertures
SYS ROUTER and PMA contains the following apertures: 512B SYS ROUTER aperture 8KB PMA aperture
- RPG, SYS ROUTER and PMA apertures are not contiguous
- SYS Router and PMA Clocks and Reset
SYS ROUTER and PMA uses single clock and reset:
- Clock: LA clock

- Hardware Performance Monitor (HWPM)
- Reset: Global HWPM reset
- Cross Triggers
PMA supports global input and output trigger. The trigger has the following characteristics:
- Trigger generation
- Initiator PMA sources trigger
- Trigger is edge-based
- Initiator can use any trigger mode and mix start, stop, and PMA pulse
- Clock domain crossing can lose triggers if triggers are issued too fast
- Initiator PMA record contains correct trigger counters (start, stop, total)
- Trigger capture
- Target PMA captures trigger
- Configure TESLA trigger mode and enable remote PMA engine trigger (start)
- Target PMA record will not have correct start and stop but will have correct total count
- Target does not need to enable PMA record. If triggers are not lost due to clock domain
crossing, then TOTAL_TRIG_RCV will match for all PERFMONs.
- Destination of PMA output global trigger is GPU PMA
### 9.12.3 Programming Guidelines
Programming of the SoC HWPM path is performed as a static configuration across all IP for a given monitoring period. HWPM context save and restore in SoC clients will not be supported. The following programming sequence is recommended for HWPM path setup and teardown:
- Setup HWPM path
- Allocate buffering for performance monitor records
- Enable clocks for IP to avoid hanging system
- Via a function call BPMP driver
-
- Program PERFMON registers for IPs being monitored
- Program PERFMUX registers for IPs being monitored
- Program HWPM SYS ROUTER and PMA
- Bind the PMA buffer
- Enable PERFMONs for IPs being monitored
- Execute use case(s)
- Post process performance monitor records
- Teardown HWPM path
- Disable PMA triggers
- Disable all PERFMONs

- Always-On Cluster (AON) and SPE
Wait for PERFMONS, ROUTER, and PMA to idle.
- Disable PMA streaming
- Toggle (on – off) global HWPM reset
- De-allocate buffers
The SoC HWPMs are programmed via the SoC HWPM driver which is separate from IP drivers. Given that both IP and SoC HWPM driver are accessing the same aperture, rules for register access must be established. Rules for IP register access in IP space are as follows:
- IP driver never accesses HWPM PERFMUX register
- Accomplished via deny-listing HWPM PERFMUX register
- HWPM SoC driver only accesses HWPM PERFMUX register
- Accomplished via allow-listing HWPM access space
The SoC HWPM software tool stack will support the following configurations:
- Temporal and range-based profiling
- Graphics and compute software stacks
- Architectural and developer tool software stacks
- SoC and GPU profiling in isolation
- SoC and GPU profiling combined
## 9.13 Always-On Cluster (AON) and SPE
### 9.13.1 Overview
The AON cluster provides a processor (the SPE) and a set of peripherals in a small power island that supports some I/O functions, and also provides hardware to wake the SoC when a number of different events occur. Use cases include, but are not limited to: low power sensor management during standby running specific communications stacks in standby, including the CAN bus wake management from sleep states The cluster consists of an ARM Cortex®-R5F processor core with a tightly coupled RAM, supporting peripherals (such as timers and an interrupt controller), various I/O controller peripherals, a DMA engine, an inter-connect fabric, and the logic to manage the cluster power states and wake sensing feature.

- Always-On Cluster (AON) and SPE
The Cortex-R5F processor in the AON cluster is also referred to as the SPE, an abbreviation for its mobile role as the Sensor Processing Engine. For brevity, the Cortex-R5F core used in AON is referred to as Cortex-R5 or just R5, but does include the Floating Point and Double Precision Floating Point capabilities. While the Cortex-R5 may be used for the use cases outlined above, it is actually a generic CPU able to run arbitrary software. Nothing in the hardware design limits usage to just those applications.
#### 9.13.1.1 AON Cluster Diagram
The following figure shows the block diagram of the AON Cluster.
**Figure 9.96 AON Cluster Block Diagram**

- AON Cluster Functional Description
### 9.13.2 AON Cluster Functional Description
#### 9.13.2.1 Cortex-R5 Processor
The Cortex-R5 processor supports the following features:
- ARMv7-R ISA
- Integrated instruction and data caches
- TCM interface for local SRAM
- Vectored interrupt support
64-bit AXI master interface for DRAM requests 32-bit AXI master interface for MMIO requests 32-bit AHB master interface for AVIC access
- AXI slave interface for DMA access to the local SRAM
#### 9.13.2.2 Processor Configuration
The AON Cluster Cortex-R5 is implemented with the following features: 32 KB instruction cache 32 KB data cache 256 KB of SRAM attached to TCMB as two banks of 128 KB SRAM
- TCMA base address set to 0
- Init low vectors
- Processor version r1p3
- Floating-Point Unit extensions including double-precision capability
#### 9.13.2.3 Interrupt Controller
The Cortex-R5 does not have an integrated interrupt controller. A pair of ARM PL192 vectored interrupt controllers on the local bus serve as the interrupt controller for the AON Cortex-R5. The SoC implements PL192 version r0p0.
#### 9.13.2.4 Processor and Interrupt Controller Documentation
Detailed documentation for the Cortex-R5 Processor and the PL192 Interrupt Controller are readily available from ARM, and may be downloaded from the ARM web site. A good starting point is these documents:

- AON Cluster Functional Description
- Document DDI 0273A
This TRM assumes access to these documents, and does not reproduce information from them.
#### 9.13.2.5 Timers
The Cortex-R5 does not have any integrated timers. Timers for the AON Cluster are provided by a separate timer block in the AON Cluster.
#### 9.13.2.6 TCM Interface
- Cortex-R5 provides two tightly coupled memory ports. These ports, named ATCM and BTCM are
meant to provide low latency access to the Cortex-R5 processor. The ATCM hosts the EVP interface (see below) and the BTCM hosts a 256 KB RAM.
**Figure 9.97 Cortex-R5 TCM interface**
The following pin and build configurations define the ATCM and BTCM configurations.

- AON Cluster Functional Description
#### 9.13.2.7 ATCM Interface
The Cortex-R5 ATCM interface hosts the Exception Vector Table module. The EVP is wrapped in the ATCM Wrapper that provides the ATCM interface to Cortex-R5.
##### 9.13.2.7.1 Exception Vector Pointers (EVP)
When Cortex-R5 takes an exception (including the reset exception), processor execution is forced to an address that corresponds to the type of exception. These addresses are called the exception vectors. By default, the exception vectors are eight consecutive word-aligned memory addresses, starting at an exception base address (which is reset default to 0x0000_0000 for the AON Cortex- R5 since VINITHI is tied to 0). The EVP module is implemented through a set of 16 registers starting at address 0x0. The table below shows the default organization of the EVP coming out of reset. The programmability of EVP allows the processor to jump to an arbitrary address space in the entire memory space that is accessible to the AON Cortex-R5.
- Table 9.98 EVP Register Table
- Offset
- Data
- Description
0x00
- LDR, PC, [PC+0x18]
- Reset vector. According to the ARM Architecture
manual, the PC reads an address equals current instruction + 8. 0x04
- LDR, PC, [PC+0x18]
- Undefined exception
0x08
- LDR, PC, [PC+0x18]
- SWI (also known as SVC) exception
0x0C
- LDR, PC, [PC+0x18]
- Prefetch abort
0x10
- LDR, PC, [PC+0x18]
- Data abort
0x14
- LDR, PC, [PC+0x18]
- Reserved
0x18
- LDR, PC, [PC+0x18]
- IRQ
0x1C
- LDR, PC, [PC+0x18]
- FIQ
0x20
- NV_ADDRESS_MAP_AON_BTCM
- TCMB Base Address
0x24
- NV_ADDRESS_MAP_AON_BTCM + 4
0x28
- NV_ADDRESS_MAP_AON_BTCM + 8
0x2C
- NV_ADDRESS_MAP_AON_BTCM + 12
0x30
- NV_ADDRESS_MAP_AON_BTCM + 16
0x34
- NV_ADDRESS_MAP_AON_BTCM + 20
0x38
- NV_ADDRESS_MAP_AON_BTCM + 24

- AON Cluster Functional Description
- Offset
- Data
- Description
0x3C
- NV_ADDRESS_MAP_AON_BTCM + 30
The EVP table above assumes that the TCMB starts at address NV_ADDRESS_MAP_AON_BTCM. The redirection of exception vectors enables BPMP to execute an ISR handler in the entire memory space accessible by it. The following steps are performed to handle EVP requests: 1. 2. The Cortex-R5 issues a read for exception vector address (@0x0000_00XX). EVP decodes exception vector aperture, and generates read-response. a. Returns LD PC, [PC + 0x18] instruction as return data. 3. This results in PC getting loaded with pointer to exception vector. Notes: 1. Cortex-R5 additionally supports vectored exception handling specifically for IRQ interrupts.
- This is supported in conjunction with the PL192 VIC interrupt controller. When an IRQ
exception is triggered by the VIC, it can optionally send out a pointer to the interrupt handler to service that particular interrupt. In case of vectored interrupts through VIC, the EVP table is not referenced by the Cortex-R5.
- EVP in earlier chips supported vectorization of interrupts. This is deprecated since the
required vectorization support is already provided through VIC interface.
- EVP reset is decoupled from the Cortex-R5 CPU reset. This enables the Cortex-R5 to be
independently reset without affecting the content of EVP registers. 2. 3.
#### 9.13.2.8 Error Checking and Signaling
When access is not allowed by the ATCM interface, it signals an error. When bit[0] of the Auxiliary Control Register (ACTLR.ATCMECEN) is set to 1, the error triggers an undefined exception on Cortex-R5 accesses or AXI slave error on AXI slave requests by asserting the ATCERROR. ATCERROR is asserted when any of the following happens:
- ATCADDR address does not decode into the address space implemented within the ATCM
wrapper. Whenever write access is made from the ARM ATCM interface to the ATCM.
- ATCLATEERROR is not used to trigger an error since the ATCLATEERROR response is
discarded by the AXI slave interface.

- AON Cluster Functional Description
#### 9.13.2.9 BTCM Interface
BTCM hosts the Cortex-R5 tightly coupled 256 KB RAM. BTCM is split internally into two ports,
- B0TCM and B1TCM. The address range for the BTCM is defined in the
NV_ADDRESS_MAP_AON_BTCM define in the global AMAP. The BTCM has two ports for two reasons:
- One can be used by the processor and the other by the AON DMA. Cortex-R5 can work on
one bank of the BTCM while AON DMA can page in data/code to/from the other.
- If the code and data space can be separated between B0TCM and B1TCM, then instruction
and data accesses from Cortex-R5 can be done independently. 1. 2.
#### 9.13.2.10 BTCM RAM Configuration
The 128 KB of RAM on each BTCM channel is constructed of eight banks of 16 KB RAM. The BTCM
- RAM banks are logically configured into eight 32 KB power-gating groups. Since the BTCM
interface is operating in interleaved mode one bank from B0TCM and one bank from B1TCM are in each power-gating group. Bank 0 starts at the bottom 32 KB of the BTCM address space.
#### 9.13.2.11 Error Checking and Signaling
Accesses to the BTCM signal an error when the particular access isn't allowed by BTCM interface.
- When bit 1 and bit 2 of the Auxiliary Control Register (ACTLR.B0TCMECEN and
ACTLR.B1TCMECEN) are set to 1, the error triggers an undefined exception on Cortex-R5 accesses or AXI slave error on AXI slave requests by asserting the B0TCERROR or B1TCERROR. An error is asserted when any of the following occurs:
- The SD, SLP, or DSLP port for a TCM RAM bank is asserted and the TCM RAM bank is
accessed.
#### 9.13.2.12 Interrupts
The interrupt sources from/to the SPE (Cortex-R5) in the AON block are comprised of the following components: {Vector/Scalar} interrupts are driven through a pair of daisy-chained ARM Vectored Interrupt
- Controllers (AVIC)
1.
- Specific peripherals such as TKE, DMA, etc., (which are exclusively owned by the SPE) have
their interrupts connected to AVIC.
- Other I/O peripherals which can be owned by the SPE and other system masters, such as
the CCPLEX, send their interrupts to AVIC as well as to top level LIC, depending on the usage model software can mask the interrupts in unused path.

- AON Cluster Functional Description
2. Interrupts from the Legacy Interrupt Controller (LIC) that feed into the AVIC.
- There are four such interrupts coming from LIC. There are a few peripherals and interrupt
sources connected to LIC can be managed by the SPE by properly enabling the routing to AON cluster channel in LIC and finally routing the aggregated interrupt to AVIC. 3.
- AON Interrupts to other Clusters
There are a few peripherals that are controlled by other clusters though they are instanced in the AON cluster (e.g., CAN, UART). For example, specific instances of the UART have usage models where they are controlled by the Safety Cluster Engine (SCE) or the Boot and
- Power Management Processor (BPMP). These peripherals interrupt are connected to the
- AON cluster and to the other clusters apart from LIC; they are fanned out to LIC and other
respective clusters. 4.
- Interrupts to AON from SoC Domain Peripherals
- UART6 and UART8 from the SoC domain send interrupts to AON cluster. These interfaces
could be repurposed as Debug UART in closed system debug; interrupts from these UART instances are routed to AVIC so that the Debug UART driver running in SPE (which various debug routines interact) is getting the correct interrupt. 5.
- Secure Interrupts Routing
- Secure interrupts are generally routed to LIC and subsequently handled by the
TrustZone Interrupt handler. Secure Interrupt is also routed as a fault to the HSM via error loggers in the AON cluster NoC.
- There are cases where the secure interrupt could be overloaded with normal functional
scenarios (e.g., APB slave indicates a security access violation, such as SCR, or other violations such as an address hole via SLAVE_ERR). If the secure interrupt become overloaded, the AON Fabric asserts the Secure Interrupt to be resolved by the TrustZone software.

- AON Cluster Functional Description
**Figure 9.98 Interrupt Structure**
Like any other APB peripherals in AON, AVIC access and configuration spaces are accessed via
- MMIO path. The AVIC port of Cortex-R5 interfaces to the PL192 and enables the following
functions in hardware to minimize software overhead of servicing the interrupt. 1. 2. 3. Determine from the interrupt controller which interrupt source is requesting service. Determine where the service routine for that interrupt source is loaded.
- Masks that interrupt source, before re-enabling processor interrupts to permit another
interrupt to be taken. Interrupt connections to PL192 include: 1.
- Local interrupts originating from within the AON cluster are connected to PL192 interrupt
inputs; these are vectored (i.e., mapped to IRQ for low interrupt latency). Four external interrupts are connected from the LIC. Each interrupt can be an aggregation of one or more interrupts. Interrupts from selective SoC peripherals like UARTs and Doorbell units. 2. 3.

- AON Cluster Functional Description
- AON Fabric
The AON cluster uses a single FlexNoC IP for routing access to DRAM via DBB and MMIO requests (via CBB to SoC peripherals and peripherals in AON).
##### 9.13.2.12.1 AON Cluster Fabric
The AON cluster fabric uses various Software entities to access various IP’s register space. The AON Cluster fabric supports: MMIO requests from various AON initiators to SoC peripherals (via CBB), and to peripherals in AON. MMIO requests from various CBB initiators to IPs in the AON cluster.
- AON DMA accesses to DRAM, AON TCMs, and various MMIO requests to IPs in AON and SoC
domains. 1. 2. 3.

- Error Generation and Logging
- Error Monitor
Each Initiator node in the fabric contains an Error Monitor Module. This module can raise an interrupt on the following errors:
- SLVERR/DECODE error
- Power down error
- Firewall error
- Timeout error
- Unsupported access error
- Error Notifier and Interrupt
- The AON Cluster fabric implements one Error Notifier module which collates interrupts from
various Error Monitor blocks in the initiator nodes and generates a single interrupt signal. This

- AON Cluster Functional Description
interrupt is routed to both LIC and SPE-AVIC. In addition, this interrupt is routed as a fault directly to the HSM.
- Error Collator
The AON cluster fabric implements error collators in each partition where the AON Fabric logic exists. Faults from the diagnostics implemented in the fabric logic sitting in each partition are routed to the respective error collators. In AON, since the fabric logic is implemented in both AOPG and AONPG partitions, four faults from AON fabric are routed to HSM (two from each EC i.e., a corrected and uncorrected fault from each error collator).
##### 9.13.2.12.2 Always-On Power Manager (AO-PM or PM)
The block called AO-PM contains registers and state machines which enable the Cortex-R5 and other resources to enter low power states when the Cortex-R5 is idle. Additionally, AO-PM also contains some general configuration registers related to the cluster.
- The AO-PM unit implements the SPE Power States and coordinates with PMC and AO-CAR to
manage the SPE and System Power State transitions. It provides a programming interface to the SPE to manage the cluster's power states. The functionalities of this module include the following: 1.
- SPE Power State Control: Making power state transition decisions according to enabled
power state configurations and triggering entry-exit sequences to/from SPE power states.
- SPE Power State exit management: Monitoring and responding to the processor complex and
DMA engine's activity requests and wake events that trigger SPE Power State exits.
- Clock source management: Sending control signals to AO CAR state machines for controlling
the AO PLL, switching clock sources for the processor complex for the target SPE power state.
- Power-gating: Handshake with PMC to support retention mode for the processor caches,
- TCMs RAMs, and the power gate-able portions of the AO cluster. Handshakes with NOC-PM
to make sure there are no pending transactions before power gating AOPG partition and ensures NOC is ready to accept transactions post PG reset deassertion.
- Power Security: Supports a secure configuration of the mapping of the clocking and power
resources per AO power state. PMC handshake: Handshake with PMC to support interaction of SPE power states with SC7.
- RTC Alarm: Implements an alarm for the SPE to be woken up from Dormant state when the
timers are power-gated. 2. 3. 4. 5. 6. 7. This module is not directly responsible for the SoC power states which are controlled by PMC.
##### 9.13.2.12.3 Use Cases for the AO-PM
1. 2. 3.
- Cortex-R5 and TCM Idle based power management
- Dynamic Clock Gating of TCM clocks
- Dynamic Power Gating of Cortex-R5 cluster, if supported

- AON Cluster Functional Description
4. 5. 6. 7.
- Cortex-R5 Clock burst policy (For AON_CPU_NIC_CLK and AON_APB_CLK) on interrupts
- SRAM low power mode controls
- Baseline support for SC7
- Boot support
##### 9.13.2.12.4 SPE Power State Management
At the core of the AO-PM Module is a Power State Transition FSM that controls transitions from SPE ACTIVE to any of the "target" low power states. These states can be SPE_IDLE, SPE_STANDBY, or SPE_DORMANT, either Shallow or Deep. Before the SPE executes WFI/WFE, it programs the power configuration registers in AO-PM to a target state, such that whenever the AO-PM detects WFI/WFE along with nCLKSTOPPED, it can readily transition to one of the targeted states. Primarily, two sets of registers must be programmed: 1. 2. TGT_PWR_STATE register (Programmed by SPE-FW).
- POWER_CFG_MASK registers (Set up by Power Security software). This set overrides the
TGT_PWR_STATE registers. Entry into any of the low-power states is possible by two conditions: 1.
- Functional Trigger from the Cortex-R5: Either nWFIPIPESTOPPED or nWFEPIPESTOPPED is
asserted, along with nCLKSTOPPED asserted.
- Debug trigger: A debug register (DBG_FEATURES_0_FORCE_START) write can also trigger a
specific state machine. This is provided for ASIC debug purposes only. 2. AOPM does not enter into the low power states if the global clock override is set. Exit conditions from low power states are: !WFI/WFE/nCLKSTOPPED
- DMA active (Wake on a transaction on AXI-S port of the Cortex-R5)
- IRQ or FIQ
- LP_EXIT trigger (register write)
- DEBUG TIMEOUT (if stuck in a state longer than timer value)
- RTC alarm
- Wake Interrupt (Tier 0)
- SC7 wake request goes high from PMC (For Tier 1 or Tier 2 wake events)
Some interrupts generated from the Cortex-R5-clusters are not routed to the local VIC but go to the LIC. These are not capable of automatically causing the PM to exit an LP state. The low power

- AON Cluster Functional Description
states lower the cluster clock frequencies and potentially slow down the interrupt service routine running on the external target processor. Such interrupts and their effects are analyzed below. 1. Watchdog_remote interrupt – This is the WDT 3rd expiry interrupt from the local TKE's WDT.
- The handler for the remote WDT wakes up the PM by issuing a software triggered exit
through the LP_EXIT register. For AON, when the expiry occurs in SC7, the 3rd expiry of SPE-
- WDT causes an SC7 exit. This results in the AOPM coming out to an ACTIVE state and then
re-entering a SHALLOW state. In the SHALLOW state, the handler for the WDT accesses the LP_EXIT register and causes a wake to ACTIVE. In order to prevent the PM from going back to a low power state, the WFI is masked by the handler inside the PM. In general, the 1st or 2nd expiry of the WDT causes an exit from the LP state – hence the 3rd expiry is not expected to occur in an LP state. However, if the 3rd expiry occurs in an LP state (or if the 1st and 2nd expiries are masked) then the handler uses the knobs described above to force the PM to exit to the low power state. HSP_SI_[1|2|3|4] interrupts – An outbound full interrupt is raised when the local processor posts a message to another processor. The local processor may execute WFI after posting the message. The PM does not need to stay in the ACTIVE state. When the target processor consumes the interrupt and clears it, the local processor does not need to move to the
- ACTIVE state. If the target processor requires further communication with the local
processor, it posts another message that causes the HSP_SI_0 interrupt to assert and wake the PM from the ACTIVE state. An outbound empty interrupt is raised when the target processor consumes the message. The interrupt requires the target processor, not the local processor, to wake to the ACTIVE state. APB bridge error and security interrupts – The effect of these interrupts not causing an LP (Low Power) state exit is to slow down the interrupt handler routine. 2. 3.
- SPE-IDLE Power State
SPE-Idle is a group of idle states characterized by the following steady state conditions:
- Cortex-R5 in Standby: nWFIPIPESTOPPED or nWFEPIPESTOPPED, and nCLKSTOPPED are
asserted. The states of the L1 caches, ASTs and external paths from the SPE cluster to SoC domain are subject to ULP mode. These are idle and not being used by the Cortex-R5 actively.
- Second level clock gating handles the SPE and TCM clocks. I/O controllers may be clock-
gated by software. DMA is idle. I/O controllers in slave mode may be active and using local buffering but those in master mode are idle. Clocks can be switched to alternative sources including OSC. Switchover to 32 kHz is allowed only when ALLOW_DEEP_I bit of AOPM_POWER_CFG_MASK_0 is set and the system is in SC7 state. The I/O controller configurations and their clock controls can be in various states depending on use cases.

- AON Cluster Functional Description
- Entry Configuration
- The TCM is clock gated on assertion of nCLKSTOPPED signal. AO-PM detects this signal to
gate and ungate the TCM clocks.
- The AO PLL may be configured by SPE-FW to be placed in a low power mode like DISABLE/
- IDDQ if the I/O controllers are under S-managed clock-gated state or if their clocks are
configured to be supplied from non-PLL sources like OSC and 32 kHz. When AO PLL is placed in a low power mode, the default clock source is switched to OSC. The switching of the clock source is handled by AO-CAR. AON Rail retention is not enabled in this state. The TCM does not expect to be in retention mode in this shallower idle state.
- BPMP and PMC control whether clocks can be switched to 32 kHz in this state when the
system is in SC7.
- SPE-Standby Power State
The SPE-Standby state is entered when the SPE subsystem has been resident in the SPE-Idle state for a programmable interval of time. SPE-Standby is similar to SPE-Idle except that the TCM and cache SRAMs are placed in a low power mode (RET). The TCMs or the caches cannot be accessed when placed in RET mode. When the RAMs are placed in RET mode, the AO rail can be externally lowered to the level of SRAM retention voltage (expected to be higher than minimum functional logic voltage)
- TCM and Cache RAMs are put in RET mode by the AOPM Standby sequencer when the RAM_RET
bit in the AOPM_TGT_PWR_STATE_0 register is set. Other cluster RAMs are placed in RET mode under software control. Clocks and AO PLL are configured the same way as for SPE-Idle. Before the TCM is placed in retention, access to the TCM controller are blocked by the AO-PM hardware. The AO-PM also resolves race conditions between entering this low power state and an exit trigger like an interrupt showing up during entry. The L1 caches can be placed in retention mode along with the TCMs. The interrupts to the Cortex-R5 are explicitly blocked when caches are placed in retention. Accesses through the AXI-S slave port are blocked by AO-PM hardware too. Exit conditions for this state are identical to SPE-Idle state. The Deep Standby state entry is allowed only if the system is in the SC7 state and the ALLOW_DEEP_S bit in the AOPM_POWER_CFG_MASK_0 register is set.
- SPE-Dormant Power State
- The SPE wakes up on a CAN wake event to process the CAN message. The hardware architecture
supports a generic partially power-gated state for the AO cluster that can also be entered more aggressively when the sensor functions are enabled in the ULP mode. This power state may also trivially be engaged when sensor functions are statically disabled in the system by configuration or by user intervention. TCMs, Caches and other RAMs in AOPG cluster can be placed in RET mode. SPE’s reset vector must point to the TCM when the processor is taken out of power-gating since the SoC domain is

- AON Cluster Functional Description
powered off during this time. When all the SRAMs in the VDD_RTC power domain are placed in RET mode, the VDD_RTC rail can be lowered to SRAM_VRET. AOPG cluster can be power gated completely. In addition to the low power states of the resources supported in SPE-Idle and SPE-Standby state, the OSC pad can be turned off in this state if the system is in SC7. This requires all clocks to be switched to 32 kHz either in software or in hardware or gated off prior to OSC is turned off. AOPM can enter its Deep Dormant state, only when SoC is in the SC7 state. The PM handshakes with the
- SC7 FSM in the PMC to ensure that the SoC is in SC7 before the PM enters the deep dormant
state. There is a software knob to bypass the second clock switch (the one that switches to 32K and turns off OSC). If the BYPASS_TWO_STEP bit is set, DEEP_DORMANT is entered after PG. (There is no retention wait counter for dormant state.) The RAM_RET and RAIL_RET options are as discussed in the STBY section. The only difference is that even if no RAM_RET is requested, hardware will do RAM_RET, but it will still not override what software has requested. Exit from the SPE-Dormant state is triggered by interrupts (in the shallow state) from sources that are not power-gated and by wake events from sources that are power-gated, including the SC7 Tier 0 wake events. Thus, exit from the SPE-Dormant is automatically included in the SC7 exit sequence.
- Exit Handling Due to SC7 Exit
When AOPM FSMs are in any non-active state, an SC7 exit of the PMC results in an exit condition of AOPM FSMs, and this exit in non-maskable. That is, unlike for other exit causes who have a mask/unmask bit in POWER_CFG_MASK register, the SC7 exit from the PMC results in AOPM exiting from LP states. While an SC7 entry of the PMC gates the entry into deep states only, an SC7 exit causes an exit whether AOPM FSMs are in their shallow or deep flavors. An SC7 exit also gates off further deep state re-entry, unless explicitly reprogrammed to do so by the BPMP before the next SC7 entry. Example: If in a deep state and an SC7 exit happens in the PMC:
- Some deep state -> (SC7 wake) -> Shallow state -> Transitions for exit -> Active -> (LP
condition) -> Go up to Shallow only If in a shallow state and an SC7 exit happens in the PMC: Some shallow state -> (SC7 wake) -> Transition for exit -> Active -> (LP condition) -> go up to
- Shallow only
- Clock Burst on Interrupt
The processor may be busy executing very low horsepower tasks but not be able to suspend all tasks and execute a WFI instruction. The processor may switch its clocks to a slow source like OSC clock. This slow clock affects the latency to a subsequent interrupt. The added latency of

- AON Cluster Functional Description
processing the interrupt signal sequence and the ISR at a slow clock prevents the processor from switching to the slow clock when it is not completely idle but very lazy. AOPM supports switching the processor subsystem clocks to a faster source automatically when an interrupt is triggered. The interrupt specific clock source is used in both active and low power idle states of the processor complex. When the processor is active, the SPE-FW requests the AO-PM to switch aon_cpu_clk, aon_nic_clk and aon_apb_clk to pre-programmed values in the AO-CAR. SPE-FW writes to AOPM_PWR_STATE register STATE = ACTIVE_IRQFIQ to raise this request. AO-PM sends a request to AO-CAR using the TGT_ACTIVE_IRQFIQ command index, waits for the AO-CAR to switch the clocks and acknowledge, after which it updates AOPM_PWR_STATUS to STATE = ACTIVE_IRQFIQ to reflect that the software request is completed. SPE-FW switches the clocks back to the TGT_ACTIVE state after servicing the pending interrupts. When SPE-FW switches the clocks back to TGT_ACTIVE state, the AOPM_PWR_STATUS gets updated to STATE = ACTIVE.
- When the processor is idle and the AO-PM has placed the subsystem in one of the low power
states described above, if the exit condition is an interrupt, the AO-PM hardware requests the AOCAR to auto-switch to the faster clock sources if BURST mode is enabled. This feature leverages the index-based request/acknowledge protocol between AOPM and the AO-
- CAR block. BURST_CLK_EN field in AOPM_BURST_CLK_0 register must be set to enable this
feature in ACTIVE state and BURST_LP_EN field of the same register must be set to enable this feature in non-ACTIVE or low power states. If the interrupt to Cortex-R5 is masked inside the PM module using the LP_EXIT_MASK register, then clock bursting is bypassed by the PM hardware upon a wake from nCLKSTOPPED or nWFIPIPESTOPPED de-assertion event.
##### 9.13.2.12.5 AO I/O DPD
AO-PM does not control any DPD control of the AO I/O pads. This is handled inside the PMC.
##### 9.13.2.12.6 SRAM Low Power Mode Management
AO-PM implements a handshake with the PMC in the Standby/Dormant states in order to put the following RAMs in retention mode.
- This interface constitutes a request from the AO-PM and an acknowledgement coming from the
PMC. For the RAMs to be put in retention, the request line is asserted HIGH and low indicates removal of Retention mode.
- In the case of NO_ACK from PMC, PM FSM times out and an interrupt can be raised on
NO_RET_ACK. The following RAMs are controlled by PMC: 1. 2.
- B0TCM and B1TCM_Bank0
- B0TCM and B1TCM_Bank1

- AON Cluster Functional Description
3. 4. 5. 6. 7. 8. 9.
- B0TCM and B1TCM_Bank2
- B0TCM and B1TCM_Bank3
- B0TCM and B1TCM_Bank4
- B0TCM and B1TCM_Bank5
- B0TCM and B1TCM_Bank6
- B0TCM and B1TCM_Bank7
- Cache0
- LCache0
- DCache0
10. 11.
- CAN0
- CAN1
In addition to the hardware handshake, shutdown and retention controls of these Cluster RAMs can be directly controlled by software and those registers lie in the PMC module. Note: CAN RAMs are not controlled by AO-PM hardware sequencer. It is mandatory for SPE-SW to put CAN RAMs in low power mode (RET or SLEEP) if AO-PM is configured to enter the deep- dormant state, as it is illegal to have RAM in functional mode and Logic in a PG-ed state. If RAM_RET == RAIL_RET == 0, then Standby FSM becomes identical to Idle FSM, barring the fact that different CAR target request ids are sent from PM to CAR.
#### 9.13.2.13 AXI Block Support
AOPM provides support via configuration registers to block AXI requests. The following register in AO-PM supports this.
- Register: AOPM_AON_BLOCK_0
Fields:
- AOPM_AON_BLOCK_0_AON2SNIC_REQ: This bit blocks AXI requests on the control fabric to
the SOC. Since AON can be operating when SOC is not powered on, it is used to block the requests going towards SOC.
- AOPM_AON_BLOCK_0_AON2MC_REQ: This bit in AO-PM blocks AXI requests to MC. Since
- AON can be operating when MC is not powered on, it is used to stop the requests going
towards SOC. Additionally, there is a status register to read back status from the AXIBLOCK FSM via a block status [31:0] sideband input to AO-PM.

- AON Cluster Functional Description
#### 9.13.2.14 Interrupts
AO-PM has three interrupts going out.
- Table 9.99 PM Interrupts
- Interrupt Sources
- Pm2vic_intr
- RTC_ALARM
- NO_CAR_ACK
- NO_OSC_ACK
- NO_RET_ACK
- NO_NOC_IDLE_ACK
- NO_NOC_IDLE
- Pm2lic_intr
- RTC_ALARM
- NO_CAR_ACK
- NO_OSC_ACK
- NO_RET_ACK
- NO_NOC_IDLE_ACK
- NO_NOC_IDLE
- Pm_actmon_intr
- PM Unit Actmon
- RTC_ALARM = When the RTC second/millisecond time matches the programmed value in the
configuration registers, it generates an RTC alarm. The RTC generates an interrupt or generate an exit from low power states. The interrupt handler for the RTA Alarm Wake reprograms the
- AOPM_RTC_ALARM_TIMER and/or AOPM_RTC_ALARM_MSEC_TIMER registers before clearing the
interrupt so that the interrupt does not fire spuriously. BPMP_PM (bpmp_pm2lic_int) and BPMP- PM (bpmp_pm2vic_int) are generated from the same sources, but have a different set of status (pre-mask), Mask, Set, and Clear registers for VIC and for LIC interrupts. Note that SPE should not be interrupted for NO_DVS_ACK. The interrupt to NO_DVS_ACK can be selectively masked off for SPE but enabled for LIC (for BPMP). NO_CAR_ACK = When CAR is requested to change the clock source or assert/de-assert PG resets, but the PM FSM times out waiting for an ACK, this interrupt is raised. The PM FSM cannot assume a good clock or a complete reset sequence, and the BPMP may need to come in and reset the PM and the system to a known good state of clocks.
- NO_OSC_ACK = When PMC has been requested to turn on/off the OSC ACK is obtained, instead
the PM FSM times out waiting for an ACK from the PMC.
- NO_RET_ACK = When PMC has been requested to put RAMs in/out retention mode, but the PM
does not get an ACK, the PM FSM times out waiting for an ACK from the PMC. In this case, PM FSM cannot assume any good state of cluster RAMs (partial sequencing of RAM low power mode is also a possible scenario). Upon narrowing down that the interrupt is due to NO_RET_ACK, software

- AON Cluster Functional Description
can check the corresponding RAM status bits and also the PG controller status register to infer if there is a hang in the PG controller in the PMC.
- NO_NOC_IDLE_ACK = When NOC_PM has been requested to query power disconnect modules/T-
NIUs that there are no pending transactions. If the PM does not get an ACK, the PM FSM timer times out.
- NO_NOC_IDLE = When NOC_PM does not give an IDLE indication for the PM dormant state
machine to move forward, the PM FSM times out.
#### 9.13.2.15 Cortex-R5 Activity Monitor
- The AO-PM has a unit ACTMON. The interrupt from the unit ACTMON is not merged with the PM
interrupt. It is separately routed to the local VIC and to LIC. The PM uses this ACTMON to detect idle periods of the processor complex and monitors key events that the Cortex-R5 exposes on its event bus. The idle state of the Cortex-R5 is not exposed to the event bus. Therefore, the idle signals nCLKSTOPPED, nWFIPIPESTOPPED, and nWFEPIPESTOPPED must be separately exported to detect idle period statistics and use the information for scaling the performance of the Cortex- R5.

- AON Cluster Functional Description
**Figure 9.99 Cortex-R5 Unit ACTMON**
- Outputs from the Cortex-R5 are sampled at the processor clock frequency and a cumulative
counter incremented for every cycle that the unit_active signal is asserted. The cumulative count is compared with a target count. When the cumulative count in processor clock rate reaches the target value, the ACTMON is notified. The EVENTBUS signals hooked up to the ACTMON are indicative of the following events.
- EVENTBUS[5]: Indicates that the instruction is architecturally executed. Asserted on every
cycle when an instruction was architecturally executed by the processor.
- EVENTBUS[16]: Stalls because instruction buffer cannot deliver an instruction. This can
indicate an instruction cache miss. Asserted every cycle where the condition is present.

- AON Cluster Functional Description
- EVENTBUS[19]: External memory request. Examples of this are cache refill, non-cacheable
accesses, write-through writes, cache line evictions (write-back).
- EVENTBUS[20]: Stalls because of Load Store Unit (LSU) being busy. The event takes place
each clock cycle where the condition is met. A high incidence of this event indicates the pipeline is often waiting for transactions to complete on the external bus.

- This address space is address decoded within PM into PM_IMPL (the functional PM part), the
PM_ACTMON (of the unit ACTMON), and the rest is reserved.
